title: Shinto

infobox.value: (神道, *Shintō*; Japanese pronunciation: [[ɕiꜜn.toː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese")[[2]](#cite_note-2)), also called **Shintoism**, is the native religion and former ethnic cult of [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"). Classified as an [East Asian religion](/wiki/East_Asian_religions "East Asian religions") by [scholars of religion](/wiki/Religious_studies "Religious studies"), it is often regarded by its practitioners as Japan's [indigenous religion](/wiki/Indigenous_religion "Indigenous religion") and as a [nature religion](/wiki/Nature_religion "Nature religion"). Scholars sometimes call its practitioners *Shintoists*, although adherents rarely use that term themselves. With no central authority in control of Shinto, there is much diversity of belief and practice evident among practitioners. As [polytheistic](/wiki/Polytheism "Polytheism") and [animistic](/wiki/Animism "Animism") religion, Shinto revolves around supernatural entities called the *[kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami")* (神), who are believed to inhabit all things, including forces of nature and prominent landscape locations.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Japanese religion

For other uses, see [Shinto (disambiguation)](/wiki/Shinto_(disambiguation) "Shinto (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Itsukushima_Gate.jpg/330px-Itsukushima_Gate.jpg)](/wiki/File:Itsukushima_Gate.jpg)

The [torii](/wiki/Torii "Torii") gateway to the [Itsukushima Shrine](/wiki/Itsukushima_Shrine "Itsukushima Shrine") in [Hiroshima Prefecture](/wiki/Hiroshima_Prefecture "Hiroshima Prefecture"), Japan, one of the most famous examples in the country.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200270,_72-1) Torii mark the entrance to Shinto shrines and are recognizable symbols of the religion.

**Shinto** (神道, *Shintō*; Japanese pronunciation: [[ɕiꜜn.toː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese")[[2]](#cite_note-2)), also called **Shintoism**, is the native religion and former ethnic cult of [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"). Classified as an [East Asian religion](/wiki/East_Asian_religions "East Asian religions") by [scholars of religion](/wiki/Religious_studies "Religious studies"), it is often regarded by its practitioners as Japan's [indigenous religion](/wiki/Indigenous_religion "Indigenous religion") and as a [nature religion](/wiki/Nature_religion "Nature religion"). Scholars sometimes call its practitioners *Shintoists*, although adherents rarely use that term themselves. With no central authority in control of Shinto, there is much diversity of belief and practice evident among practitioners. As [polytheistic](/wiki/Polytheism "Polytheism") and [animistic](/wiki/Animism "Animism") religion, Shinto revolves around supernatural entities called the *[kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami")* (神), who are believed to inhabit all things, including forces of nature and prominent landscape locations.

The *kami* are worshipped at *[kamidana](/wiki/Kamidana "Kamidana")* household shrines, family shrines, and [*jinja* public shrines](/wiki/Shinto_shrine "Shinto shrine"). The latter are staffed by priests, known as *[kannushi](/wiki/Kannushi "Kannushi")*, who oversee offerings of food and drink to the specific *kami* enshrined at that location. This is done to cultivate harmony between humans and *kami* and to solicit the latter's blessing. Other common rituals include the *[kagura](/wiki/Kagura "Kagura")* dances, [rites of passage](/wiki/Rites_of_passage "Rites of passage"), and [*kami* festivals](/wiki/Japanese_festivals "Japanese festivals"). Public shrines facilitate forms of [divination](/wiki/Divination "Divination") and supply religious objects, such as [amulets](/wiki/Amulet "Amulet"), to the religion's adherents. Shinto places a major conceptual focus on ensuring purity, largely by cleaning practices such as ritual washing and bathing, especially before worship. Little emphasis is placed on specific moral codes or particular afterlife beliefs, although the dead are deemed capable of becoming *kami*. The religion has no single creator or specific doctrine, and instead exists in a diverse range of local and regional forms.

Although historians debate at what point it is suitable to refer to Shinto as a distinct religion, *kami* veneration has been traced back to Japan's [Yayoi period](/wiki/Yayoi_period "Yayoi period") (300 BCE to 250 CE). [Buddhism](/wiki/Chinese_Buddhism "Chinese Buddhism") entered Japan at the end of the [Kofun period](/wiki/Kofun_period "Kofun period") (300 to 538 CE) and spread rapidly. [Religious syncretization](/wiki/Syncretism "Syncretism") made *kami* worship and Buddhism functionally inseparable, a process called *[shinbutsu-shūgō](/wiki/Shinbutsu-sh%C5%ABg%C5%8D "Shinbutsu-shūgō")*. The *kami* came to be viewed as part of [Buddhist cosmology](/wiki/Buddhist_cosmology "Buddhist cosmology") and were increasingly depicted [anthropomorphically](/wiki/Anthropomorphism "Anthropomorphism"). The earliest written tradition regarding *kami* worship was recorded in the 8th-century *[Kojiki](/wiki/Kojiki "Kojiki")* and *[Nihon Shoki](/wiki/Nihon_Shoki "Nihon Shoki")*. In ensuing centuries, *shinbutsu-shūgō* was adopted by Japan's Imperial household. During the [Meiji era](/wiki/Meiji_era "Meiji era") (1868 to 1912), Japan's [nationalist](/wiki/Japanese_nationalism "Japanese nationalism") leadership expelled Buddhist influence from *kami* worship and formed [State Shinto](/wiki/State_Shinto "State Shinto"), which some historians regard as the origin of Shinto as a distinct religion. Shrines came under growing government influence, and citizens were encouraged to worship the [emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan") as a *kami*. With the formation of the [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") in the early 20th century, Shinto was exported to other areas of East Asia. Following Japan's defeat in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Shinto was formally [separated from the state](/wiki/Secular_state "Secular state").

Shinto is primarily found in Japan, where there are around 100,000 public shrines, although practitioners are also found abroad. Numerically, it is Japan's largest religion, the second being Buddhism. Most of the country's population takes part in both Shinto and Buddhist activities, especially festivals, reflecting a common view in [Japanese culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Japan "Culture of Japan") that the beliefs and practices of different religions do not need to be exclusive. Aspects of Shinto have been incorporated into various [Japanese new religious movements](/wiki/Japanese_new_religions "Japanese new religions").

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/YobitoTorii.jpg/250px-YobitoTorii.jpg)](/wiki/File:YobitoTorii.jpg)

A torii gateway to the Yobito Shrine (*Yobito-jinja*) in Abashiri City, [Hokkaido](/wiki/Hokkaido "Hokkaido")

There is no universally agreed definition of Shinto.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997viiiRots2015211-3) According to Joseph Cali and John Dougill, if there was "one single, broad definition of Shinto" that could be put forward, it would be that "Shinto is a belief in *[kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami")*", the supernatural entities at the centre of the religion.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201313-4) The Japanologist [Helen Hardacre](/wiki/Helen_Hardacre "Helen Hardacre") wrote that "Shinto encompasses doctrines, institutions, ritual, and communal life based on *kami* worship",[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre20171-5) while the scholar of religion [Inoue Nobutaka](/wiki/Inoue_Nobutaka "Inoue Nobutaka") observed that the term "Shinto" was "often used" in "reference to *kami* worship and related theologies, rituals and practices".[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInoue20031-6) Various scholars have referred to practitioners of Shinto as *Shintoists*, although this term has no direct translation in the [Japanese language](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language").[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken1994xviii-7)

Scholars have debated at what point in history it is legitimate to start talking about Shinto as a specific phenomenon. The scholar of religion [Ninian Smart](/wiki/Ninian_Smart "Ninian Smart") suggested that one could "speak of the *kami* religion of Japan, which lived symbiotically with organized Buddhism, and only later was institutionalized as Shinto."[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmart1998135-8) While several institutions and practices now associated with Shinto existed in Japan by the 8th century,[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201718-9) various scholars have argued that Shinto as a distinct religion was essentially "invented" during the 19th century, in Japan's [Meiji era](/wiki/Meiji_era "Meiji era").[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen20107-10) The scholar of religion Brian Bocking stressed that, especially when dealing with periods before the Meiji era, the term *Shinto* should "be approached with caution".[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997174-11) Inoue Nobutaka stated that "Shinto cannot be considered as a single religious system that existed from the ancient to the modern period",[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInoue20035-12) while the historian [Kuroda Toshio](/wiki/Kuroda_Toshio "Kuroda Toshio") noted that "before modern times Shinto did not exist as an independent religion".[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuroda19813-13)

### Categorisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Categorisation")]

Many scholars describe Shinto as a [religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion"),[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken1994xviiNelson199626-14) a term first translated into Japanese as *shūkyō* around the time of the [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration").[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAzegami201268-15) Some practitioners instead view Shinto as a "way",[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken1994xxivCaliDougill201313-16) thus characterising it more as custom or [tradition](/wiki/Tradition "Tradition"),[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreen201069-17) partly as an attempt to circumvent the modern [separation of religion and state](/wiki/Separation_of_religion_and_state "Separation of religion and state") and restore Shinto's historical links with the Japanese state.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken1994xxiv–xxv-18) Moreover, many of the categories of religion and religiosity defined in Western culture "do not readily apply" to Shinto.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken1994xix-19) Unlike religions familiar in Western countries, such as [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") and [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), Shinto has no single founder,[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979191Littleton20026Picken20111CaliDougill201313-20) nor any single canonical text.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979191-21) Western religions tend to stress exclusivity, but in Japan, it has long been considered acceptable to practice different religious traditions simultaneously.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken1994xxx-22) Japanese religion is therefore highly [pluralistic](/wiki/Religious_pluralism "Religious pluralism").[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201148-23) Shinto is often cited alongside [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") as one of Japan's two main religions,[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill20137-24) and the two often differ in focus, with Buddhism emphasising the idea of the cessation of suffering, while Shinto focuses on adapting to life's pragmatic requirements.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199630Littleton200210-25) Shinto has integrated elements from religions imported from mainland Asia, such as Buddhism, [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism"), [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism"), and [Chinese divination](/wiki/Chinese_fortune_telling "Chinese fortune telling") practices,[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa1987139CaliDougill201313-26) and shares features like its polytheism with other [East Asian religions](/wiki/East_Asian_religions "East Asian religions").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInoue20037-27)

> Some scholars suggest we talk about types of Shintō such as popular Shintō, folk Shintō, domestic Shintō, sectarian Shintō, imperial house Shintō, shrine Shintō, state Shintō, new Shintō religions, etc. rather than regard Shintō as a single entity. This approach can be helpful but begs the question of what is meant by 'Shintō' in each case, particularly since each category incorporates or has incorporated Buddhist, Confucian, Taoist, folk religious and other elements.

— Scholar of religion Brian Bocking[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997173–174-28)

Scholars of religion have debated how to classify Shinto. Inoue considered it part of "the family of East-Asian religions".[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInoue200310-29) The philosopher [Stuart D. B. Picken](/wiki/Stuart_D._B._Picken "Stuart D. B. Picken") suggested that Shinto be classed as a [world religion](/wiki/World_religion "World religion"),[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken1994xxv-30) while the historian [H. Byron Earhart](/wiki/H._Byron_Earhart "H. Byron Earhart") called it a "major religion".[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart200431-31) Shinto is also often described as an [indigenous religion](/wiki/Indigenous_religion "Indigenous religion"),[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuroda19811Nelson19967Rots2015211-32) although this generates debates over the different definitions of "indigenous" in the Japanese context.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson19967-33) The notion of Shinto as Japan's "indigenous religion" stemmed from the growth of modern nationalism between the [Edo](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period") and Meiji periods;[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuroda198119-34) this view promoted the idea that Shinto's origins were prehistoric and that it represented something like the "underlying will of Japanese culture".[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuroda19811–2-35) The prominent Shinto theologian Sokyo Ono, for instance, said *kami* worship was "an expression" of the Japanese "native racial faith which arose in the mystic days of remote antiquity" and that it was "as indigenous as the people that brought the Japanese nation into existence".[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa1987xviii-36) Many scholars regard this classification as inaccurate. Earhart noted that Shinto, in having absorbed much Chinese and Buddhist influence, was "too complex to be labelled simply [as an] indigenous religion".[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart200431-31) In the early 21st century it became increasingly common for practitioners to call Shinto a [nature religion](/wiki/Nature_religion "Nature religion"),[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERots2015210-37) which critics saw as a strategy to disassociate the tradition from controversial issues surrounding militarism and imperialism.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERots2015210-37)

Shinto displays substantial local variation;[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979215-38) the anthropologist John K. Nelson noted it was "not a unified, monolithic entity".[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson19967-33) [Different types of Shinto](/wiki/Shinto_sects_and_schools "Shinto sects and schools") have been identified. "Shrine Shinto" refers to practices centred around shrines,[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979192Nelson19967-39) and "Domestic Shinto" to the veneration of *kami* in the home.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979192-40) Some scholars have used the term "Folk Shinto" to designate localised Shinto practices,[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201189CaliDougill201314-41) or practices outside of an institutionalised setting.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson19967-33) In various past eras, there was also a "[State Shinto](/wiki/State_Shinto "State Shinto")", in which Shinto beliefs and practices were closely interlinked with the Japanese state.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979192Nelson19967-39) In representing "a portmanteau term" for many varied traditions across Japan, the term "Shinto" is similar to the term "[Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism")", used to describe varied traditions across South Asia.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997viii-42)

### Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Etymology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Takachiho-gawara_Kirishima_City_Kagoshima_Pref02n4050.jpg/250px-Takachiho-gawara_Kirishima_City_Kagoshima_Pref02n4050.jpg)](/wiki/File:Takachiho-gawara_Kirishima_City_Kagoshima_Pref02n4050.jpg)

A torii gate at the [Takachiho-gawara](/wiki/Takachiho-gawara "Takachiho-gawara") shrine near [Kirishima](/wiki/Kirishima,_Kagoshima "Kirishima, Kagoshima"), [Kagoshima Prefecture](/wiki/Kagoshima_Prefecture "Kagoshima Prefecture"), which is associated with the mythological tale of [Ninigi-no-Mikoto](/wiki/Ninigi-no-Mikoto "Ninigi-no-Mikoto")'s [descent to earth](/wiki/Tenson_k%C5%8Drin "Tenson kōrin").

The term *Shinto* is often broadly translated into English as "the way of the *kami*",[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979193Kitagawa1987139Bocking1997173Nelson200014Earhart20042Picken20119-43) although its meaning has varied throughout Japanese history.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuroda19814Bocking1997viii,_173-44) Other terms are sometimes used synonymously with "Shinto"; these include **kami no michi** (神の道, "the way of the *kami*"), **kannagara no michi** (神ながらの道, also written 随神の道 or 惟神の道, "the way of the *kami* from time immemorial"), **Kodō** (古道, "the ancient way"), **Daidō** (大道, "the great way"), and **Teidō** (帝道, "the imperial way").[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken1994xxivPicken201164-45)

The term *Shinto* derives from the combination of two Chinese characters: *[shin](/wiki/Shen_(Chinese_religion) "Shen (Chinese religion)")* (神), which means "spirit" or "god", and *[tō](/wiki/Dao "Dao")* (道), which means "way", "road" or "path".[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa1987139Littleton20026Picken20119-46) "Shintō" (神道, "the Way of the Gods") was a term already used in the *[Book of Changes](/wiki/Book_of_Changes "Book of Changes")* referring to the divine order of nature.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Around the time of the spread of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") in the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") (206 BCE – 220 CE), it was used to distinguish [indigenous Chinese religions](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion") from the imported religion. [Ge Hong](/wiki/Ge_Hong "Ge Hong") used it in his *[Baopuzi](/wiki/Baopuzi "Baopuzi")* as a synonym for [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism").[[48]](#cite_note-48)

The [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") term 神道 ([MC](/wiki/Middle_Chinese "Middle Chinese") *zyin dawX*) was originally adopted into Japanese as *Jindō*;[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETeeuwen2002243-49) this was possibly first used as a Buddhist term to refer to non-Buddhist deities.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETeeuwen2002256-50) Among the earliest known appearances of the term *Shinto* in Japan is in the 8th-century text, *[Nihon Shoki](/wiki/Nihon_Shoki "Nihon Shoki")*.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETeeuwen2002236Hardacre201741-51) Here, it may be a generic term for popular belief,[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuroda19814–5Teeuwen2002237-52) or alternatively reference Taoism, as many Taoist practices had recently been imported from mainland Asia.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuroda19816Teeuwen2002237Hardacre201742-53) In these early Japanese uses, the word *Shinto* did not apply to a distinct religious tradition nor to anything uniquely Japanese;[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuroda19817-54) the 11th century *[Konjaku monogatarishui](/wiki/Konjaku_Monogatarish%C5%AB "Konjaku Monogatarishū")* for instance refers to a woman in China practicing *Shinto*, and also to people in India worshipping *kami*, indicating these terms were being used to describe religions outside Japan itself.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuroda19819–10-55)

In medieval Japan, *kami*-worship was generally seen as being part of [Japanese Buddhism](/wiki/Japanese_Buddhism "Japanese Buddhism"), with the *kami* themselves often interpreted as [Buddhas](/wiki/Buddhas "Buddhas").[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuroda198111,_12-56) At this point, the term *Shinto* increasingly referred to "the authority, power, or activity of a *kami*, being a *kami*, or, in short, the state or attributes of a *kami*."[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuroda198110-57) It appears in this form in texts such as **Nakatomi no harai kunge** and *[Shintōshū](/wiki/Shint%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB "Shintōshū")* tales.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuroda198110-57) In the *[Japanese Portuguese Dictionary](/wiki/Nippo_Jisho "Nippo Jisho")* of 1603, *Shinto* is defined as referring to "*kami* or matters pertaining to *kami*."[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuroda198110–11-58) The term *Shinto* became common in the 15th century.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201742-59) During the late Edo period, the *[kokugaku](/wiki/Kokugaku "Kokugaku")* scholars began using the term *Shinto* to describe what they believed was an ancient, enduring and indigenous Japanese tradition that predated Buddhism; they argued that *Shinto* should be used to distinguish *kami* worship from traditions like Buddhism, Taoism, and Confucianism.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuroda198119Bocking1997174-60) This use of the term *Shinto* became increasingly popular from the 18th century.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997174-11) The term *Shinto* has been commonly used only since the early 20th century, when it superseded the term *taikyō* ('great religion') as the name for the Japanese state religion.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997viii-42) In English, the religion is also called "Shintoism",[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) although some scholars have argued against the inclusion of the suffix *[-ism](/wiki/-ism "-ism")* due to Shinto's lack of codified doctrine.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJensenBlok2013110-64)[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoveday2019104-65)

## Beliefs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Beliefs")]

### *Kami*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Kami")]

Main article: [Kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/A_man_confronted_with_an_apparition_of_the_Fox_goddess.jpg/250px-A_man_confronted_with_an_apparition_of_the_Fox_goddess.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_man_confronted_with_an_apparition_of_the_Fox_goddess.jpg)

An artistic depiction by [Utagawa Kuniyoshi](/wiki/Utagawa_Kuniyoshi "Utagawa Kuniyoshi") of the *kami* Inari appearing to a man

Shinto is [polytheistic](/wiki/Polytheism "Polytheism"), involving the veneration of many deities known as *kami*,[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200223CaliDougill201313-66) or sometimes as *jingi* (神祇).[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199770Hardacre201731-67) In Japanese, no distinction is made here between singular and plural, and hence the term *kami* refers both to individual *kami* and the collective group of *kami*.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoydWilliams200535CaliDougill201313-68) Although lacking a direct English translation,[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart20048-69) the term *kami* has sometimes been rendered as "god" or "spirit".[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart20042CaliDougill201313-70) The historian of religion [Joseph Kitagawa](/wiki/Joseph_Kitagawa "Joseph Kitagawa") deemed these English translations "quite unsatisfactory and misleading",[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa198736-71) and various scholars urge against translating *kami* into English.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979194Bocking199784-72) In Japanese, it is often said that there are eight million *kami*, a term which connotes an infinite number,[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199629Littleton200224-73) and Shinto practitioners believe that they are present everywhere.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre20171-5) They are not regarded as [omnipotent](/wiki/Omnipotence "Omnipotence"), [omniscient](/wiki/Omniscience "Omniscience"), or necessarily [immortal](/wiki/Immortality "Immortality").[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoydWilliams200535Hardacre201752-74)

The term *kami* is "conceptually fluid",[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoydWilliams200535-75) being "vague and imprecise".[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979194-76) In Japanese it is often applied to the power of phenomena that inspire a sense of wonder and awe in the beholder.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken1994xxiBoydWilliams200535-77) Kitagawa referred to this as "the *kami* nature", stating that he thought it "somewhat analogous" to the Western ideas of the [numinous](/wiki/Numinous "Numinous") and the [sacred](/wiki/Sacred "Sacred").[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa198736-71) *Kami* are seen to inhabit both the living and the dead, organic and inorganic matter, and natural disasters like earthquakes, droughts, and plagues;[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201313-4) their presence is seen in natural forces such as the wind, rain, fire, and sunshine.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201314-78) Accordingly, Nelson commented that Shinto regards "the *actual phenomena* of the world itself" as being "divine".[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199626-79) This perspective has been characterised as being [animistic](/wiki/Animism "Animism").[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson19967Picken201140CaliDougill201313-80)

In Japan, *kami* have been venerated since prehistory.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre20171-5) During the [Yayoi period](/wiki/Yayoi_period "Yayoi period") they were regarded as being formless and invisible,[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201719-81) later coming to be depicted anthropomorphically under Buddhist influence.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997180Hardacre20171-82) Now, statues of the *kami* are known as *shinzo*.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997180-83) *Kami* are usually associated with a specific place, often a prominent landscape feature such as a waterfall, mountain, large rock, or distinctive tree.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200275CaliDougill201314-84) Physical objects or places in which the *kami* are believed to have a presence are termed *[shintai](/wiki/Shintai "Shintai")*;[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997172-85) objects inhabited by the *kami* that are placed in the shrine are known as *go-shintai*.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979202Nelson1996144-86) Objects commonly chosen for this purpose include mirrors, swords, stones, beads, and inscribed tablets.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979202Earhart200436–37-87) These *go-shintai* are concealed from the view of visitors,[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979202Picken201144-88) and may be hidden inside boxes so that even the priests do not know what they look like.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997172-85)

*Kami* are deemed capable of both benevolent and destructive deeds;[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199627CaliDougill201313-89) if warnings about good conduct are ignored, the *kami* can mete out punishment, often illness or sudden death, called *shinbatsu*.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997164-90) Some *kami*, referred to as the *magatsuhi-no-kami* or *araburu kami*, are regarded as malevolent and destructive.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997114Picken201142-91) Offerings and prayers are given to the *kami* to gain their blessings and to dissuade them from destructive actions.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201313-4) Shinto seeks to cultivate and ensure a harmonious relationship between humans and the *kami* and thus with the natural world.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart20047–8-92) More localised *kami* may be subject to feelings of intimacy and familiarity from members of the local community that are not directed towards more widespread *kami* like Amaterasu.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199633-93) The *kami* of a particular community is referred to it as their *ujigami*,[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997214–215Littleton200224-94) while that of a particular house is the *yashikigami*.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997222-95)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Takeo_Shrine_Sacred_tree.jpg/250px-Takeo_Shrine_Sacred_tree.jpg)](/wiki/File:Takeo_Shrine_Sacred_tree.jpg)

A 3000 year old sacred tree ([shintai](/wiki/Shintai "Shintai")) of Takeo Shrine

*Kami* are not considered [metaphysically](/wiki/Metaphysics "Metaphysics") different from humanity,[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoydWilliams200535-75) with it being possible for humans to become *kami*.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart20048-69) Ancestors and other dead humans are sometimes venerated as *kami*, being regarded as protectors.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200227CaliDougill201313Hardacre20171-96) For example, [Emperor Ōjin](/wiki/Emperor_%C5%8Cjin "Emperor Ōjin") was posthumously enshrined as the *kami* [Hachiman](/wiki/Hachiman "Hachiman"), believed to be a protector of Japan and a *kami* of war.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200231–32CaliDougill201314-97) In Western Japan, the term *[jigami](/wiki/Jigami "Jigami")* is used to describe the enshrined *kami* of a village founder.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199769-98) In some cases, living human beings were also viewed as *kami*;[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201313-4) these were called *akitsumi kami*[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201135–36-99) or *arahito-gami*.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201142-100) In the State Shinto system of the Meiji era, the emperor of Japan was declared to be a *kami*,[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart20048-69) while several Shinto sects have also viewed their leaders as living *kami*.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart20048-69)

Although some *kami* are venerated only in a single location, others have shrines across many areas.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201315-101) Hachiman for instance has around 25,000 shrines dedicated to him,[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201314-78) while Inari has 40,000.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201192-102) The act of establishing a new shrine to a *kami* who already has one is called *[bunrei](/wiki/Bunrei "Bunrei")* ("dividing the spirit").[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199713Picken201157CaliDougill201315-103) As part of this, the *kami* is invited to enter a new place, with the instalment ceremony known as a *[kanjo](/wiki/Kanjo "Kanjo")*.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201315-101) The new, subsidiary shrine is known as a *bunsha*.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199713Picken201158-104) Individual *kami* are not believed to have their power diminished by their residence in multiple locations, and there is no limit on the number of places a *kami* can be enshrined.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201315-101) In some periods, fees were charged for the right to enshrine a particular *kami* in a new place.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201315-101) Shrines are not necessarily always designed as permanent structures.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre20171-5)

Many *kami* have messengers, known as *kami no tsukai* or *tsuka washime*, that generally take animal forms.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201315-101) Inari's messenger, for example, is a fox (*[kitsune](/wiki/Kitsune "Kitsune")*),[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201140CaliDougill201315-105) while Hachiman's is a dove.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201315-101)
Shinto cosmology also includes spirits who cause malevolent acts, *[bakemono](/wiki/Bakemono "Bakemono")*, a category including *[oni](/wiki/Oni "Oni")*, *[tengu](/wiki/Tengu "Tengu")*, *[kappa](/wiki/Kappa_(folklore) "Kappa (folklore)")*, *[mononoke](/wiki/Mononoke "Mononoke")*, and *[yamanba](/wiki/Yama-uba "Yama-uba")*.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking19978-106) Japanese folklore also incorporates belief in the *[goryō](/wiki/Gory%C5%8D "Goryō")* or *[onryō](/wiki/Onry%C5%8D "Onryō")*, unquiet or vengeful spirits, particularly of those who died violently and without appropriate funerary rites.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199737-107) These are believed to inflict suffering on the living, meaning that they must be pacified, usually through Buddhist rites but sometimes through enshrining them as a *kami*.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199737-107) Other Japanese supernatural figures include the *[tanuki](/wiki/Bake-danuki "Bake-danuki")*, animal-like creatures who can take human form.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997200-108)

### Cosmogony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Cosmogony")]

Main articles: [Amenominakanushi](/wiki/Amenominakanushi "Amenominakanushi") and [Japanese creation myth](/wiki/Japanese_creation_myth "Japanese creation myth")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Kobayashi_Izanami_and_Izanagi.jpg/250px-Kobayashi_Izanami_and_Izanagi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kobayashi_Izanami_and_Izanagi.jpg)

[Izanami](/wiki/Izanami "Izanami")-no-Mikoto and [Izanagi](/wiki/Izanagi "Izanagi")-no-Mikoto, by Kobayashi Eitaku, late 19th century

Although the narratives differ in detail,[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201748–49-109) the origin of the *kami* and of Japan itself are recounted in two 8th-century texts, *[Kojiki](/wiki/Kojiki "Kojiki")* and *[Nihon Shoki](/wiki/Nihon_Shoki "Nihon Shoki")*.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979195Kitagawa1987142Littleton200223Earhart200432CaliDougill201318-110) Drawing heavily on Chinese influence,[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979195Kitagawa1987142Littleton200237Earhart200433-111) these texts were commissioned by ruling elites to legitimize and consolidate their rule.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart200433–34CaliDougill201318–19-112) Although never of great importance to Japanese religious life,[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart200433-113) in the early 20th century the government proclaimed that their accounts were factual.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201319-114)

The *Kojiki* recounts that the universe started with *ame-tsuchi*, the separation of light and pure elements (*ame*, "heaven") from heavy elements (*tsuchi*, "earth").[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking19975Picken201138CaliDougill201319-115) Three *kami* then appeared: [Amenominakanushi](/wiki/Amenominakanushi "Amenominakanushi"), [Takamimusuhi no Mikoto](/wiki/Takamimusubi "Takamimusubi"), and [Kamimusuhi no Mikoto](/wiki/Kamimusubi "Kamimusubi"). Other *kami* followed, including a brother and sister, [Izanagi](/wiki/Izanagi "Izanagi") and [Izanami](/wiki/Izanami "Izanami").[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201319Hardacre201748-116) The *kami* instructed Izanagi and Izanami to create land on earth. To this end, the siblings stirred the briny sea with a jewelled spear, from which [Onogoro Island](/wiki/Onogoro_Island "Onogoro Island") was formed.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa1987143CaliDougill201319–20Hardacre201749-117) Izanagi and Izanami then descended to Earth, where the latter gave birth to further *kami*. One of these was a fire *kami*, whose birth killed Izanami.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa1987143CaliDougill201320Hardacre201750-118) Izanagi descended to *[yomi](/wiki/Yomi "Yomi")* to retrieve his sister, but there he saw her body putrefying. Embarrassed to be seen in this state, she chased him out of *yomi*, and he closed its entrance with a boulder.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa1987143Bocking199767CaliDougill201320Hardacre201750-119)

Izanagi bathed in the sea to rid himself from the pollution brought about by witnessing Izanami's putrefaction. Through this act, further *kami* emerged from his body: [Amaterasu](/wiki/Amaterasu "Amaterasu") (the sun *kami*) was born from his left eye, [Tsukuyomi](/wiki/Tsukuyomi "Tsukuyomi") (the moon *kami*) from his right eye, and [Susanoo](/wiki/Susanoo "Susanoo") (the storm *kami*) from his nose.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979196Kitagawa1987143Bocking199767CaliDougill201320Hardacre201753-120) Susanoo behaved in a destructive manner, to escape him Amaterasu hid herself within a cave, plunging the earth into darkness. The other *kami* eventually succeeded in coaxing her out.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979196–197Kitagawa1987144Bocking19973CaliDougill201321Hardacre201753–54-121) Susanoo was then banished to earth, where he married and had children.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201322Hardacre201754-122) According to the *Kojiki*, Amaterasu then sent her grandson, [Ninigi](/wiki/Ninigi "Ninigi"), to rule Japan, giving him curved beads, a mirror, and a sword: the symbols of Japanese imperial authority.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa1987144Hardacre201757-123) Amaterasu remains probably Japan's most venerated *kami*.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200298-124)

### Cosmology and afterlife

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Cosmology and afterlife")]

In Shinto, the creative principle permeating all life is known as *musubi*, and is associated with its own *kami*.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997129BoydWilliams200534-125) Within traditional Japanese thought, there is no concept of an overarching duality between good and evil.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200226Picken201136-126) The concept of *aki* encompasses misfortune, unhappiness, and disaster, although it does not correspond precisely with the Western concept of evil.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201136-127) There is no [eschatology](/wiki/Eschatology "Eschatology") in Shinto.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201171-128) Texts such as the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki* portray multiple realms in Shinto cosmology.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoerner1977153–154-129) These present a universe divided into three parts: the Plane of High Heaven (*[Takama-no-hara](/wiki/Takamagahara "Takamagahara")*), where the *kami* live; the Phenomenal or Manifested World (*[Utsushi-yo](/wiki/Ashihara_no_Nakatsukuni "Ashihara no Nakatsukuni")*), where humans dwell; and the Nether World (*Yomotsu-kuni*), where unclean spirits reside.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa1987143Bocking1997216-130) The mythological texts nevertheless do not draw firm demarcations between these realms.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa1987143-131)

Modern Shinto places greater emphasis on this life than on any afterlife,[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoerner1977153Littleton200290-132) although it does espouse belief in a human spirit or soul, the *mitama* or *tamashii*, which contains four aspects.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201775-133) While indigenous ideas about an afterlife were probably well-developed prior to Buddhism's arrival,[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200290-134) contemporary Japanese people often adopt Buddhist afterlife beliefs.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200289-135)
Mythological stories like the *Kojiki* describe *yomi* or *yomi-no-kuni* as a realm of the dead,[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200290Picken201171-136) although this plays no role in modern Shinto.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200290-134) Modern Shinto ideas about the afterlife largely revolve around the idea that the spirit survives bodily death and continues to assist the living. After 33 years, it then becomes part of the family *kami*.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200289–91-137) These ancestral spirits are sometimes thought to reside in the mountains,[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200291Picken201139-138) from where they descend to take part in agricultural events.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201139-139) Shinto's afterlife beliefs also include the *obake*, restless spirits who died in bad circumstances and often seek revenge.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200292-140)

### Purity and impurity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Purity and impurity")]

A key theme in Shinto is the avoidance of *[kegare](/wiki/Kegare "Kegare")* ("pollution" or "impurity"),[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199793CaliDougill201320-141) while ensuring *[harae](/wiki/Harae "Harae")* ("purity").[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996101Bocking199745CaliDougill201321-142) In Japanese thought, humans are seen as fundamentally pure.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201145,_82-143) *Kegare* is therefore seen as being a temporary condition that can be corrected through achieving *harae*.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199793-144) Rites of purification are conducted so as to restore an individual to "spiritual" health and render them useful to society.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996102-145)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Karasuzumo_purification_ritual.jpg/250px-Karasuzumo_purification_ritual.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karasuzumo_purification_ritual.jpg)

Shinto purification rite after a ceremonial children's [sumo](/wiki/Sumo "Sumo") tournament at the [Kamigamo Jinja](/wiki/Kamigamo_Jinja "Kamigamo Jinja") in [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto")

This notion of purity is present in many facets of Japanese culture, such as the focus it places on bathing.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199638-146) Purification is for instance regarded as important in preparation for the planting season,[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199663-147) while performers of *[noh](/wiki/Noh "Noh")* theatre undergo a purification rite before they carry out their performances.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken20117-148) Among the things regarded as particular pollutants in Shinto are death, disease, witchcraft, the flaying alive of an animal, incest, bestiality, excrement, and blood associated with either menstruation or childbirth.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979206Nelson1996104-149) To avoid *kegare*, priests and other practitioners may engage in abstinence and avoid various activities prior to a festival or ritual.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199793Picken201186-150)
Various words, termed *imi-kotoba*, are also regarded as taboo, and people avoid speaking them when at a shrine; these include *shi* (death), *byō* (illness), and *shishi* (meat).[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199758-151)

A purification ceremony known as *misogi* involves the use of fresh water, salt water, or salt to remove *kegare*.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997124-152) Full immersion in the sea is often regarded as the most ancient and efficacious form of purification.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996140-153) This act links with the mythological tale in which Izanagi immersed himself in the sea to purify himself after discovering his deceased wife; it was from this act that other *kami* sprang from his body.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996141Bocking1997124-154) An alternative is immersion beneath a waterfall.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997124Picken201145-155) Salt is often regarded as a purifying substance;[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996141Earhart200411-156) some Shinto practitioners will for instance sprinkle salt on themselves after a funeral,[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996141–142Picken201170-157) while those running restaurants may put a small pile of salt outside before business commences each day.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken20116-158) Fire, also, is perceived as a source of purification.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart200411-159) The *yaku-barai* is a form of harae designed to prevent misfortune,[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997219-160) while the *oharae*, or "ceremony of great purification", is often used for end-of-year purification rites, and is conducted twice a year at many shrines.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997136-161)

### *Kannagara*, morality, and ethics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Kannagara, morality, and ethics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Yasukuni_Shrine_2012.JPG/250px-Yasukuni_Shrine_2012.JPG)](/wiki/File:Yasukuni_Shrine_2012.JPG)

The actions of priests at the Yasukuni Shrine in Tokyo have generated controversy across East Asia

Shinto incorporates morality tales and myths but no codified ethical doctrine,[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201313-4) and thus no "unified, systematized code of behaviour".[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979191-21) An ethical system nevertheless arises from its practice,[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201180–81-162) with emphasis placed on sincerity (*makoto*),[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken1994xxiiiBocking1997115Picken201182-163) honesty (*tadashii*),[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken1994xxiii-164) hard work (*tsui-shin*),[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201183-165) and thanksgiving (*kansha*) directed towards the *kami*.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201183-165) *Shojiki* is regarded as a virtue, encompassing honesty, uprightness, veracity, and frankness.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997182-166) Shinto sometimes includes reference to four virtues known as the *akaki kiyoki kokoro* or *sei-mei-shin*, meaning "purity and cheerfulness of heart", which are linked to the state of *harae*.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997157Picken201134,_82-167) Attitudes to sex and fertility tend to be forthright in Shinto.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201184–85-168) Shinto's flexibility regarding morality and ethics has been a target of frequent criticism, especially from those arguing that the religion can readily become a pawn for those wishing to use it to legitimise their authority and power.[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996198-169)

In Shinto, *kannagara* ("way of the *kami*") is the law of the [natural order](/wiki/Cosmos "Cosmos"),[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken1994xxiii-164) with *wa* ("benign harmony") being inherent in all things.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200258-170) Disrupting *wa* is deemed bad, while contributing to it is thought good;[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200258,_61-171) as such, subordination of the individual to the larger social unit has long been a characteristic of the religion.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200211,_57-172) Throughout Japanese history, the notion of *saisei-itchi*, or the union of religious authority and political authority, has long been prominent.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa1987xvii-173) In the modern world, Shinto has tended toward conservatism,[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201310-174) as well as nationalism,[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997ixCaliDougill201310-175) an association that results in various Japanese [civil liberties](/wiki/Civil_liberties "Civil liberties") groups and neighboring countries regarding Shinto suspiciously.[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997ix-176) Particularly controversial has been the [Yasukuni Shrine](/wiki/Yasukuni_Shrine "Yasukuni Shrine") in Tokyo, devoted to Japan's war dead. In 1979 it enshrined 14 men who had been declared Class-A defendants at the 1946 [Tokyo War Crimes Trials](/wiki/Tokyo_War_Crimes_Trials "Tokyo War Crimes Trials"), generating domestic and international condemnation, particularly from China and Korea.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson200012Littleton200299Picken201118–19-177)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Hushimi-inari-taisha_otsuka3.jpg/250px-Hushimi-inari-taisha_otsuka3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hushimi-inari-taisha_otsuka3.jpg)

Assemblage of small *torii* at the Fushimi Inari-taisha shrine in Kyoto

Shinto priests face ethical conundrums. In the 1980s, for instance, priests at the [Suwa Shrine](/wiki/Suwa_Shrine_(Nagasaki) "Suwa Shrine (Nagasaki)") in [Nagasaki](/wiki/Nagasaki "Nagasaki") debated whether to invite the crew of a U.S. Navy vessel docked at the port city to their festival celebrations given the sensitivities surrounding the [1945 U.S. use of the atomic bomb on the city](/wiki/Atomic_bombings_of_Hiroshima_and_Nagasaki#Nagasaki "Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki").[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199666–67-178) In other cases, priests have opposed construction projects on shrine-owned land;[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUeda1979317Rots2015221-179) at [Kaminoseki](/wiki/Kaminoseki "Kaminoseki") in the early 2000s, a priest was pressured to resign after opposing the sale of shrine lands to build a [nuclear power plant](/wiki/Nuclear_power_plant "Nuclear power plant").[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERots2015221-180)
In the 21st century, Shinto has increasingly been portrayed as a nature-centred spirituality with [environmentalist](/wiki/Environmentalism "Environmentalism") credentials;[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERots2015205,_207-181) several shrines have collaborated with local environmentalist campaigns,[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERots2015223-182) while an international interfaith conference on environmental sustainability was held at the Ise shrine in 2014.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERots2015205–206-183) Critical commentators have characterised the presentation of Shinto as an environmentalist movement as a rhetorical ploy rather than a concerted effort by Shinto institutions to become environmentally sustainable.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERots2015208-184)

## Practices

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Practices")]

Shinto [focuses on ritual behavior rather than doctrine](/wiki/Orthopraxy "Orthopraxy").[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979214CaliDougill201310-185) The philosophers James W. Boyd and Ron G. Williams stated that Shinto is "first and foremost a ritual tradition",[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoydWilliams200533-186) while Picken observed that "Shinto is interested not in *credenda* but in *agenda*, not in things that should be believed but in things that should be done."[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken1994xxxii-187) The scholar of religion Clark B. Offner stated that Shinto's focus was on "maintaining communal, ceremonial traditions for the purpose of human (communal) well-being".[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979198-188)
It is often difficult to distinguish Shinto practices from Japanese customs more broadly,[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill20138-189) with Picken observing that the "worldview of Shinto" provided the "principal source of self-understanding within the Japanese way of life".[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken1994xxxii-187) Nelson stated that "Shinto-based orientations and values [...] lie at the core of Japanese culture, society, and character".[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson19963-190)

### *Jinja* shrines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Jinja shrines")]

Main article: [Shinto shrine](/wiki/Shinto_shrine "Shinto shrine")

See also: [Shinto architecture](/wiki/Shinto_architecture "Shinto architecture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Fushimi_Inari_-_Main_gate.jpg/250px-Fushimi_Inari_-_Main_gate.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fushimi_Inari_-_Main_gate.jpg)

The main gate to [Fushimi Inari-taisha](/wiki/Fushimi_Inari-taisha "Fushimi Inari-taisha") in Kyoto, one of the oldest shrines in Japan

Public spaces in which the *kami* are worshipped are often known under the generic term *[jinja](/wiki/Shinto_shrine "Shinto shrine")* ("*kami*-place");[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken1994xviiiBocking199772Earhart200436CaliDougill20137-191) this term applies to the location rather than to a specific building.[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201121-192) *Jinja* is usually translated as "shrine" in English,[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart200436-193) although in earlier literature was sometimes translated as "temple",[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken1994xviii-7) a term now more commonly reserved for Japan's Buddhist structures.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart200436BreenTeeuwen20101-194) There are around 100,000 public shrines in Japan;[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen20101-195) about 80,000 are affiliated with the [Association of Shinto Shrines](/wiki/Association_of_Shinto_Shrines "Association of Shinto Shrines"),[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken1994xxxiPicken201129BreenTeeuwen20105CaliDougill20138-196) with another 20,000 being unaffiliated.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201129-197) They are found all over the country, from isolated rural areas to dense metropolitan ones.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart200436CaliDougill20137-198) More specific terms are sometimes used for certain shrines depending on their function; some of the grand shrines with imperial associations are termed *jingū*,[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199771,_72-199) those devoted to the war dead are termed *shokonsha*,[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997182-166) and those linked to mountains deemed to be inhabited by *kami* are *yama-miya*.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997220-200)

Jinja typically consist of complexes of multiple buildings,[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200268-201) with the architectural styles of shrines having largely developed by the [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period").[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199693-202) The inner sanctuary in which the *kami* lives is the *[honden](/wiki/Honden "Honden")*.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199692Littleton200272Picken201143CaliDougill20137-203) Inside the *honden* may be stored material belonging to the *kami*; known as *shinpo*, this can include artworks, clothing, weapons, musical instruments, bells, and mirrors.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997170-204) Typically, worshippers carry out their acts outside of the *honden*.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill20137-24) Near the *honden* can sometimes be found a subsidiary shrine, the *bekkū*, to another *kami*; the *kami* inhabiting this shrine is not necessarily perceived as being inferior to that in the *honden*.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking19979-205) At some places, halls of worship have been erected, termed *[haiden](/wiki/Haiden_(Shinto) "Haiden (Shinto)")*.[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199692Bocking199742Picken201143CaliDougill20137-206) On a lower level can be found the hall of offerings, known as a *[heiden](/wiki/Heiden_(Shinto) "Heiden (Shinto)")*.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199692Bocking199749Picken201143-207) Together, the building housing the *honden*, *haiden*, and *heiden* is called a *hongū*.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199754-208) In some shrines, there is a separate building in which to conduct additional ceremonies, such as weddings, known as a *gishikiden*,[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199734-209) or a specific building in which the *kagura* dance is performed, known as the *kagura-den*.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199782-210) Collectively, the central buildings of a shrine are known as the *shaden*,[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997160-211) while its precincts are known as the *keidaichi*[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199794-212) or *shin'en*.[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997166-213) This precinct is surrounded by the *tamagaki* fence,[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997197-214) with entry via a *shinmon* gate, which can be closed at night.[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997169-215)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Plan_of_Shinto_Shrine.jpg/330px-Plan_of_Shinto_Shrine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Plan_of_Shinto_Shrine.jpg)

Diagram of a *jinja*: 1. *[torii](/wiki/Torii "Torii")*, 2. stone stairs, 3. *[sandō](/wiki/Sand%C5%8D "Sandō")*, 4. *[chōzuya](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Dzuya "Chōzuya")*, 5. *[tōrō](/wiki/T%C5%8Dr%C5%8D "Tōrō")*, 6. *[kagura-den](/wiki/Kagura-den "Kagura-den")*, 7. *shamusho*, 8. *[ema](/wiki/Ema_(Shint%C5%8D) "Ema (Shintō)")*, 9. [*Sessha*/*massha*](/wiki/Setsumatsusha "Setsumatsusha"), 10. *[komainu](/wiki/Komainu "Komainu")*, 11. *[Haiden](/wiki/Haiden_(Shinto) "Haiden (Shinto)")*, 12. *[tamagaki](/wiki/Tamagaki "Tamagaki")*, 13. *[honden](/wiki/Honden "Honden")*

Shrine entrances are marked by a two-post gateway with either one or two crossbeams atop it, known as *[torii](/wiki/Torii "Torii")*.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979201Bocking1997207Earhart200436CaliDougill20137-216) The exact details of these *torii* varies and there are at least twenty different styles.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997207Picken201143-217) These are regarded as demarcating the area where the *kami* resides;[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill20137-24) passing under them is often viewed as a form of purification.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979201-218) More broadly, *torii* are internationally recognised symbols of Japan.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill20137-24) Their architectural form is distinctly Japanese, although the decision to paint most of them in [vermillion](/wiki/Vermillion "Vermillion") reflects a Chinese influence dating from the [Nara period](/wiki/Nara_period "Nara period").[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201120-219) Also set at the entrances to many shrines are *[komainu](/wiki/Komainu "Komainu")*, statues of lion or dog like animals perceived to scare off malevolent spirits;[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979201Bocking1997104-220) typically these will come as a pair, one with its mouth open, the other with its mouth closed.[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997104-221)

Shrines are often set within gardens[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201312-222) or wooded groves called *chinju no mori* ("forest of the tutelary" *kami*),[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERots2015211-223) which vary in size from just a few trees to sizeable areas of woodland.[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERots2015219-224) Large lanterns, known as *[tōrō](/wiki/T%C5%8Dr%C5%8D "Tōrō")*, are often found within these precincts.[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997208-225) Shrines often have an office, known as a *shamusho*,[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199671Bocking199772-226) a *saikan* where priests undergo forms of abstinence and purification prior to conducting rituals,[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997148-227) and other buildings such as a priests' quarters and a storehouse.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979201-218) Various kiosks often sell amulets to visitors.[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199772–73-228) Since the late 1940s, shrines have had to be financially self-sufficient, relying on the donations of worshippers and visitors. These funds are used to pay the wages of the priests, to finance the upkeep of the buildings, to cover the shrine's membership fees of various regional and national Shinto groups, and to contribute to disaster relief funds.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199677-229)

In Shinto, it is seen as important that the places in which *kami* are venerated be kept clean and not neglected.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201123-230) Through to the Edo period, it was common for *kami* shrines to be demolished and rebuilt at a nearby location in order to remove any pollutants and ensure purity.[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199692-231) This has continued into recent times at certain sites, such as the Ise Grand Shrine, which is moved to an adjacent site every two decades.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199693Bocking1997163Nelson20004Hardacre201779–80-232) Separate shrines can also be merged in a process known as *jinja gappei*,[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199773-233) while the act of transferring the *kami* from one building to another is called *sengu*.[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997158-234) Shrines may have legends about their foundation, which are known as *en-gi*. These sometimes also record miracles associated with the shrine.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199726-235) From the Heian period on, the *en-gi* were often retold on picture scrolls known as *[emakimono](/wiki/Emakimono "Emakimono")*.[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199726Picken201144-236)

#### Priesthood and *miko*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Priesthood and miko")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Miwa-shrine_Yutateshinji_A.JPG/250px-Miwa-shrine_Yutateshinji_A.JPG)](/wiki/File:Miwa-shrine_Yutateshinji_A.JPG)

*Yutateshinji* ceremony performed by Shinto priests at the Miwa shrine in [Fuefuki, Yamanashi](/wiki/Fuefuki,_Yamanashi "Fuefuki, Yamanashi")

Shrines may be cared for by priests, by local communities, or by families on whose property the shrine is found.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill20137-24) Shinto priests are known in Japanese as *[kannushi](/wiki/Kannushi "Kannushi")*, meaning "proprietor of *kami*",[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199788-237) or alternatively as *shinshoku* or *[shinkan](/wiki/Shinkan_(official) "Shinkan (official)")*.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997168,_171-238) Many *kannushi* take on the role in a line of hereditary succession traced down specific families.[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUeda1979325Nelson199629-239) In contemporary Japan, there are two main training universities for those wishing to become *kannushi*, at [Kokugakuin University](/wiki/Kokugakuin_University "Kokugakuin University") in Tokyo and at [Kogakkan University](/wiki/Kogakkan_University "Kogakkan University") in [Mie Prefecture](/wiki/Mie_Prefecture "Mie Prefecture").[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199629Bocking199799,_102-240) Priests can rise through the ranks over the course of their careers.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199642-241) The number of priests at a particular shrine can vary; some shrines can have dozens, and others have none, instead being administered by local lay volunteers.[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200273Picken201131–32-242) Some priests administer to multiple small shrines, sometimes over ten.[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201132-243)

Priestly regalia is largely based on the clothes worn at the imperial court during the Heian period.[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson200015-244) It includes a tall, rounded hat known as an *eboshi*,[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199725-245) and black lacquered wooden clogs known as *asagutsu*.[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking19977Picken201144-246) The outer garment worn by a priest, usually colored black, red, or light blue, is the *hō*,[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199753-247) or the *ikan*.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199758-151) A white silk version of the *ikan*, used for formal occasions, is known as the *saifuku*.[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199758,_146-248) Another priestly robe is the *kariginu*, which is modelled on Heian-style hunting garments.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199789–90-249) Also part of standard priestly attire is a *hiōgi* fan,[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199751-250) while during rituals, priests carry a flat piece of wood known as a *[shaku](/wiki/Shaku_(ritual_baton) "Shaku (ritual baton)")*.[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997162-251) This regalia is generally more ornate than the sombre garments worn by Japanese Buddhist monks.[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson200015-244)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Kamogawa_ceremony_02.jpg/250px-Kamogawa_ceremony_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kamogawa_ceremony_02.jpg)

Miko performing a Shinto ceremony near the [Kamo River](/wiki/Kamo_River "Kamo River")

The chief priest at a shrine is the *gūji*.[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979212Nelson1996186Bocking199739BoydWilliams200533-252) Larger shrines may also have an assistant head priest, the *gon-gūji*.[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979212-253) As with teachers, instructors, and Buddhist clergy, Shinto priests are often referred to as *[sensei](/wiki/Sensei "Sensei")* by lay practitioners.[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996179-254) Historically, there were female priests although they were largely pushed out of their positions in 1868.[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996123-255) During the Second World War, women were again allowed to become priests to fill the void caused by large numbers of men being enlisted in the military.[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996124-256) By the late 1990s, around 90% of priests were male, 10% female,[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200298-124) contributing to accusations that Shinto discriminates against women.[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201184-257) Priests are free to marry and have children.[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996124-256) At smaller shrines, priests often have other full-time jobs, and serve only as priests during special occasions.[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979212-253)
Before certain major festivals, priests may undergo a period of abstinence from sexual relations.[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199643-258) Some of those involved in festivals also abstain from a range of other things, such as consuming tea, coffee, or alcohol, immediately prior to the events.[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996141-259)

The priests are assisted by *jinja miko*, sometimes referred to as "shrine-maidens" in English.[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997121-260) These *[miko](/wiki/Miko "Miko")* are typically unmarried,[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199647Bocking1997121-261) although not necessarily virgins.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199647-262) In many cases they are the daughters of a priest or a practitioner.[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997121-260) They are subordinate to the priests in the modern shrine hierarchy.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996124–125-263) Their most important role is in the *[kagura](/wiki/Kagura "Kagura")* dance, known as *otome-mai*.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996125-264) *Miko* receive only a small salary but gain respect from members of the local community and learn skills such as cooking, calligraphy, painting, and etiquette which can benefit them when later searching for employment or a marriage partner.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996125-264) They generally do not live at the shrines.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996125-264) Sometimes they fill other roles, such as being secretaries in the shrine offices or clerks at the information desks, or as waitresses at the *naorai* feasts. They also assist *kannushi* in ceremonial rites.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996125-264)

#### Visits to shrines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Visits to shrines")]

Visits to the shrine are termed *sankei*,[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997152-265) or *jinja mairi*.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201311-266) Some individuals visit the shrines daily, often on their morning route to work;[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201311-266) they typically take only a few minutes.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201311-266) Usually, a worshipper will approach the honden, placing a monetary offering in a box and then ringing a bell to call the *kami*'s attention.[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979201–202Littleton200272CaliDougill201311-267) Then, they bow, clap, and stand while silently offering a prayer.[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979204BreenTeeuwen20103CaliDougill201311-268) The clapping is known as *kashiwade* or *[hakushu](/wiki/Hakushu_(Shinto) "Hakushu (Shinto)")*;[[269]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199743,_90-269) the prayers or supplications as *kigan*.[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199796-270) This individual worship is known as *hairei*.[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199742-271) More broadly, ritual prayers to the *kami* are called *[norito](/wiki/Norito "Norito")*,[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997135-272) while the coins offered are *saisen*.[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997149-273) At the shrine, individuals offering prayers are not necessarily praying to a specific *kami*.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201311-266) A worshipper may not know the name of a *kami* residing at the shrine nor how many *kami* are believed to dwell there.[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979202CaliDougill201311-274) Unlike in certain other religions, Shinto shrines do not have weekly services that practitioners are expected to attend.[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart200412-275)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Shinto_Priest_Blessing_a_Car_at_the_Hokkaido_Shrine.jpg/250px-Shinto_Priest_Blessing_a_Car_at_the_Hokkaido_Shrine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shinto_Priest_Blessing_a_Car_at_the_Hokkaido_Shrine.jpg)

A [Toyota Estima](/wiki/Toyota_Previa "Toyota Previa") being blessed at the [Hokkaidō Shrine](/wiki/Hokkaid%C5%8D_Shrine "Hokkaidō Shrine") in a *kotsu anzen harai* rite

Some Shinto practitioners do not offer their prayers to the *kami* directly, but rather request that a priest offer them on their behalf; these prayers are known as *kitō*.[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199798-276) Many individuals approach the *kami* asking for pragmatic requests.[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996116-277) Requests for rain, known as *amagoi* ("rain-soliciting") have been found across Japan, with Inari a popular choice for such requests.[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking19973Picken201136-278)
Other prayers reflect more contemporary concerns. For instance, people may ask that the priest approaches the *kami* so as to purify their car in the hope that this will prevent it from being involved in an accident; the *kotsu anzen harai* ("purification for road safety").[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996116Bocking1997114Picken201188-279) Similarly, transport companies often request purification rites for new buses or airplanes which are about to go into service.[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997108Picken201188-280) Before a building is constructed, it is common for either private individuals or the construction company to employ a Shinto priest to come to the land being developed and perform the *jichinsai*, or earth sanctification ritual. This purifies the site and asks the *kami* to bless it.[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996190–196Bocking199768Picken201188-281)

People often ask the *kami* to help offset inauspicious events that may affect them. For instance, in Japanese culture, the age 33 is seen as being unlucky for women and the age 42 for men, and thus people can ask the *kami* to offset any ill-fortune associated with being this age.[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996183-282) Certain directions can also be seen as being inauspicious for certain people at certain times and thus people can approach the *kami* asking them to offset this problem if they have to travel in one of these unlucky directions.[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996183-282)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/%E7%86%B1%E7%94%B0%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE.jpg/250px-%E7%86%B1%E7%94%B0%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E7%86%B1%E7%94%B0%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE.jpg)

Torii of Atsuta Jingū

[Pilgrimage](/wiki/Pilgrimage "Pilgrimage") has long been important in Japanese religion,[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa1987xvii–xviii-283) with pilgrimages to Shinto shrines called *[junrei](/wiki/Junrei "Junrei")*.[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199780-284) A round of pilgrimages, whereby individuals visit a series of shrines and other sacred sites that are part of an established circuit, is known as a *junpai*.[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199780-284) An individual leading these pilgrims, is sometimes termed a *sendatsu*.[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997158-234) For many centuries, people have also visited the shrines for primarily cultural and recreational reasons, as opposed to spiritual ones.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201311-266) Many of the shrines are recognised as sites of historical importance and some are classified as [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Sites "World Heritage Sites").[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201311-266) Shrines such as [Shimogamo Jinja](/wiki/Shimogamo_Jinja "Shimogamo Jinja") and [Fushimi Inari Taisha](/wiki/Fushimi_Inari_Taisha "Fushimi Inari Taisha") in Kyoto, [Meiji Jingū](/wiki/Meiji_Jing%C5%AB "Meiji Jingū") in Tokyo, and [Atsuta Jingū](/wiki/Atsuta_Jing%C5%AB "Atsuta Jingū") in Nagoya are among Japan's most popular tourist sites.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERots2015221-180) Many shrines have a unique rubber-stamp seal which visitors can get printed into their stamp book, demonstrating the different shrines they have visited.[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997192-285)

### *Harae* and *hōbei*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Harae and hōbei")]

Main article: [Harae](/wiki/Harae "Harae")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/ItsukushimaBasin7406.jpg/250px-ItsukushimaBasin7406.jpg)](/wiki/File:ItsukushimaBasin7406.jpg)

Shinto rituals begin with a process of purification, often involving the washing of the hands and mouth at the *temizu* basin; this example is at Itsukushima Jinja.

Shinto rituals begin with a process of purification, or *harae*.[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199639Bocking199745-286) Using fresh water or salt water, this is known as *misogi*.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997124-152) At shrines, this entails sprinkling this water onto the face and hands, a procedure known as *temizu*,[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199745-287) using a font known as a *[temizuya](/wiki/Temizuya "Temizuya")*.[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199691-288) Another form of purification at the start of a Shinto rite entails waving a white paper streamer or wand known as the *haraigushi*.[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199639,_46Bocking199745-289) When not in use, the *haraigushi* is usually kept in a stand.[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199745-287) The priest waves the *haraigushi* horizontally over a person or object being purified in a movement known as *sa-yu-sa* ("left-right-left").[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199745-287) Sometimes, instead of a *haraigushi*, the purification is carried out with an *o-nusa*, a branch of evergreen to which strips of paper have been attached.[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199745-287) The waving of the *haraigushi* is often followed by an additional act of purification, the *shubatsu*, in which the priest sprinkles water, salt, or brine over those assembled from a wooden box called the *'en-to-oke* or *magemono*.[[290]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997184-290)

The acts of purification accomplished, petitions known as *norito* are spoken to the *kami*.[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199640-291) This is followed by an appearance by the *miko*, who commence in a slow circular motion before the main altar.[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199640-291) Offerings are then presented to the *kami* by being placed on a table.[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199640-291) This act is known as *hōbei*;[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199753-247) the offerings themselves as *saimotsu*[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997148-227) or *sonae-mono*.[[292]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997187-292) Historically, the offerings given the *kami* included food, cloth, swords, and horses.[[293]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201313–14-293) In the contemporary period, lay worshippers usually give gifts of money to the *kami* while priests generally offer them food, drink, and sprigs of the sacred *[sakaki](/wiki/Sakaki "Sakaki")* tree.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201314-78) [Animal sacrifices](/wiki/Animal_sacrifice "Animal sacrifice") are not considered appropriate offerings, as the shedding of blood is seen as a polluting act that necessitates purification.[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199664-294) The offerings presented are sometimes simple and sometimes more elaborate; at the Grand Shrine of Ise, for instance, 100 styles of food are laid out as offerings.[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199640-291) The choice of offerings will often be tailored to the specific *kami* and occasion.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997170-204)

Offerings of food and drink are specifically termed *shinsen*.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997170-204)
[Sake](/wiki/Sake "Sake"), or rice wine, is a very common offering to the *kami*.[[295]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997150-295) After the offerings have been given, people often sip rice wine known as *o-miki*.[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199640-291) Drinking the *o-miki* wine is seen as a form of communion with the *kami*.[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199653-296) On important occasions, a feast is then held, known as *naorai*, inside a banquet hall attached to the shrine complex.[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199640,_53-297)

The *kami* are believed to enjoy music.[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199649-298) One style of music performed at shrines is *[gagaku](/wiki/Gagaku "Gagaku")*.[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199649Bocking199733-299) Instruments used include three reeds (*fue*, *sho*, and *hichiriki*), the *yamato-koto*, and the "three drums" (*taiko*, *kakko*, and *shōko*).[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199733-300) Other musical styles performed at shrines can have a more limited focus. At shrines such as [Ōharano Shrine](/wiki/%C5%8Charano_Shrine "Ōharano Shrine") in Kyoto, *azuma-asobi* ("eastern entertainment") music is performed on 8 April.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking19978-106) Also in Kyoto, various festivals make use of the *[dengaku](/wiki/Dengaku "Dengaku")* style of music and dance, which originated from rice-planting songs.[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199722-301) During rituals, people visiting the shrine are expected to sit in the *[seiza](/wiki/Seiza "Seiza")* style, with their legs tucked beneath their bottom.[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996214-302) To avoid cramps, individuals who hold this position for a lengthy period of time may periodically move their legs and flex their heels.[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996214–215-303)

### Home shrines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Home shrines")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Kamidana.jpg/250px-Kamidana.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kamidana.jpg)

A *kamidana* displaying a *[shimenawa](/wiki/Shimenawa "Shimenawa")* and *[shide](/wiki/Shide_(Shinto) "Shide (Shinto)")*

Having seen their popularity increase in the Meiji era,[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199785-304) many Shinto practitioners also have a family shrine, or *[kamidana](/wiki/Kamidana "Kamidana")* ("*kami* shelf"), in their home.[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979200Nelson1996184Littleton200273Earhart200411-305) These usually consist of shelves placed at an elevated position in the living room.[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979200–201-306) *Kamidana* can also be found in workplaces, restaurants, shops, and ocean-going ships.[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199785Earhart200411-307) Some public shrines sell entire *kamidana*.[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201131-308)

Along with the *kamidana*, many Japanese households also have *[butsudan](/wiki/Butsudan "Butsudan")*, Buddhist altars enshrining the ancestors of the family;[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199713Earhart200411-309) ancestral reverence remains an important aspect of Japanese religious tradition.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201139-139) In the rare instances where Japanese individuals are given a Shinto funeral rather than a Buddhist one, a *tama-ya*, *mitama-ya*, or *sorei-sha* shrine may be erected in the home in place of a *butsudan*. This will be typically placed below the *kamidana* and include symbols of the resident ancestral spirit, for instance a mirror or a scroll.[[310]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997198-310)
