*Kamidana* often enshrine the *kami* of a nearby public shrine as well as a tutelary *kami* associated with the house's occupants or their profession.[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199785-304) They can be decorated with miniature *torii* and *shimenawa* and include amulets obtained from public shrines.[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199785-304) They often contain a stand on which to place offerings;[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979201-218) daily offerings of rice, salt, and water are placed there, with sake and other items also offered on special days.[[311]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199785Littleton200274-311) These domestic rituals often take place early in the morning,[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200281-312) and prior to conducting them, practitioners often bathe, rinse their mouth, or wash their hands as a form of purification.[[313]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979203-313)

Household Shinto can focus attention on the *dōzoku-shin*, *kami* who are perceived to be ancestral to the *dōzoku* or extended kinship group.[[314]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199724Picken201175–76-314) A small shrine for the ancestors of a household are known as *soreisha*.[[292]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997187-292) Small village shrines containing the tutelary *kami* of an extended family are known as *iwai-den*.[[315]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199766-315) In addition to the *jinja* shrines and the household shrines, Shinto also features small wayside shrines known as *[hokora](/wiki/Hokora "Hokora")*.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199754-208) Other open spaces used for the worship of *kami* are *[iwasaka](/wiki/Iwasaka "Iwasaka")*, an area surrounded by sacred rocks.[[316]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199765-316)

### *Ema*, divination, and amulets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Ema, divination, and amulets")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Ema_of_Izumo_taisha.jpg/250px-Ema_of_Izumo_taisha.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ema_of_Izumo_taisha.jpg)

A selection of wooden *ema* hanging up at a Shinto shrine

A common feature of Shinto shrines is the provision of *[ema](/wiki/Ema_(Shinto) "Ema (Shinto)")*, small wooden plaques onto which practitioners will write a wish or desire that they would like to see fulfilled. The practitioner's message is written on one side of the plaque, while on the other is usually a printed picture or pattern related to the shrine itself.[[317]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199725–26-317) *Ema* are provided both at Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan;[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199725-245) unlike most amulets, which are taken away from the shrine, the *ema* are typically left there as a message for the resident *kami*.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199726-235) Those administering the shrine will then often burn all of the collected *ema* at new year.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199726-235)

Divination is the focus of many Shinto rituals,[[318]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201318-318) with various forms of divination used by its practitioners, some introduced from China.[[319]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201173-319) Among the ancient forms of divination found in Japan are *rokuboku* and *kiboku*.[[320]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill201317-320) Several forms of divination entailing [archery](/wiki/Archery "Archery") are also practiced in Shintō, known as *[yabusame](/wiki/Yabusame "Yabusame")*, *omato-shinji*, and *mato-i*.[[321]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201143,_73-321) Kitagawa stated that there could be "no doubt" that various types of "shamanic diviners" played a role in early Japanese religion.[[322]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa198739-322) A form of divination previously common in Japan was *[bokusen](/w/index.php?title=Bokusen&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bokusen (page does not exist)")* or *uranai*, which often used tortoise shells; it is still used in some places.[[323]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201150-323)

A form of divination that is popular at Shinto shrines are the *[omikuji](/wiki/Omikuji "Omikuji")*.[[324]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997138Picken201174-324) These are small slips of paper which are obtained from the shrine (for a donation) and which are then read to reveal a prediction for the future.[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997137–138-325) Those who receive a bad prediction often then tie the *omikuji* to a nearby tree or frame set up for the purpose. This act is seen as rejecting the prediction, a process called *sute-mikuji*, and thus avoiding the misfortune it predicted.[[326]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997139Picken201174-326)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Hiromine-jinja_Omikuji.jpg/250px-Hiromine-jinja_Omikuji.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hiromine-jinja_Omikuji.jpg)

A frame at a shrine where omikuji are tied

The use of [amulets](/wiki/Amulet "Amulet") are widely sanctioned and popular in Japan.[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart200412-275) These may be made of paper, wood, cloth, metal, or plastic.[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart200412-275)
*[Ofuda](/wiki/Ofuda "Ofuda")* act as amulets to keep off misfortune and also serve as talismans to bring benefits and good luck.[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997135-272) They typically comprise a tapering piece of wood onto which the name of the shrine and its enshrined *kami* are written or printed. The *ofuda* is then wrapped inside white paper and tied up with a colored thread.[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997135–136-327) *Ofuda* are provided both at Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples.[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997135-272) Another type of amulet provided at shrines and temples are the *[omamori](/wiki/Omamori "Omamori")*, which are traditionally small, brightly colored drawstring bags with the name of the shrine written on it.[[328]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997138-328) *Omamori* and *ofuda* are sometimes placed within a charm bag known as a *[kinchaku](/wiki/Kinchaku "Kinchaku")*, typically worn by small children.[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199796-270)

At new year, many shrines sell *[hamaya](/wiki/Hama_Yumi "Hama Yumi")* (an "evil-destroying arrows"), which people can purchase and keep in their home over the coming year to bring good luck.[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199743–44-329)
A *[daruma](/wiki/Daruma_doll "Daruma doll")* is a round, paper doll of the Indian monk, [Bodhidharma](/wiki/Bodhidharma "Bodhidharma"). The recipient makes a wish and paints one eye; when the goal is accomplished, the recipient paints the other eye. While this is a Buddhist practice, darumas can be found at shrines, as well. These dolls are very common.[[330]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-330)
Other protective items include *dorei*, which are earthenware bells that are used to pray for good fortune. These bells are usually in the shapes of the zodiacal animals.[[330]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-330) *Inuhariko* are paper dogs that are used to induce and to bless good births.[[330]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-330) Collectively, these talismans through which home to manipulate events and influence spirits, as well as related mantras and rites for the same purpose, are known as *majinai*.[[331]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997114–15-331)

### *Kagura*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Kagura")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Yamanashi-oka_shrine_Daidai_Kagura_A.jpg/250px-Yamanashi-oka_shrine_Daidai_Kagura_A.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yamanashi-oka_shrine_Daidai_Kagura_A.jpg)

A *kagura* traditional dance performed at the Yamanashi-oka shrine

*[Kagura](/wiki/Kagura "Kagura")* describes the music and dance performed for the *kami*;[[332]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979205Bocking199781-332) the term may have originally derived from *kami no kura* ("seat of the *kami*").[[333]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKobayashi19813-333) Throughout Japanese history, dance has played an important culture role and in Shinto it is regarded as having the capacity to pacify *kami*.[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa198723-334) There is a [mythological](/wiki/Mythology "Mythology") tale of how *kagura* dance came into existence. According to the *Kojiki* and the *Nihon Shoki*, [Ame-no-Uzume](/wiki/Ame-no-Uzume "Ame-no-Uzume") performed a dance to entice Amaterasu out of the cave in which she had hidden herself.[[335]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa198723Bocking199781Picken201168-335)

There are two broad types of kagura.[[336]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199781-336) One is Imperial kagura, also known as *mikagura*. This style was developed in the imperial court and is still performed on [imperial grounds](/wiki/Three_Palace_Sanctuaries "Three Palace Sanctuaries") every December.[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199781–82-337) It is also performed at the Imperial harvest festival and at major shrines such as Ise, [Kamo](/wiki/Kamo_Shrines "Kamo Shrines"), and [Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū](/wiki/Iwashimizu_Shrine "Iwashimizu Shrine"). It is performed by singers and musicians using *[shakubyoshi](/wiki/Shakubyoshi "Shakubyoshi")* wooden clappers, a *[hichiriki](/wiki/Hichiriki "Hichiriki")*, a *kagura-bue* flute, and a six-stringed zither.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199782-210) The other main type is *sato-kagura*, descended from *mikagura* and performed at shrines across Japan. Depending on the style, it is performed by *miko* or by actors wearing masks to portray various mythological figures.[[338]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199782,_155-338) These actors are accompanied by a *hayashi* band using flutes and drums.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199782-210) There are also other, regional types of kagura.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199782-210)

### Festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Festivals")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Aoi_Matsuri.jpg/250px-Aoi_Matsuri.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aoi_Matsuri.jpg)

Participants in a procession for Aoi Matsuri in Kyoto

Public festivals are commonly termed *[matsuri](/wiki/Matsuri "Matsuri")*,[[339]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200281BoydWilliams200536Picken20119,_86-339) although this term has varied meanings—"festival", "worship", "celebration", "rite", or "prayer"—and no direct translation into English.[[340]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997117-340) Picken suggested that the festival was "the central act of Shinto worship" because Shinto was a "community- and family-based" religion.[[341]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken1994xxvi-341) Most mark the seasons of the agricultural year and involve offerings being directed to the *kami* in thanks.[[342]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997117–118Picken201186-342) According to a traditional [lunar calendar](/wiki/Lunar_calendar "Lunar calendar"), Shinto shrines should hold their festival celebrations on *hare-no-hi* or "clear days", the days of the new, full, and half moons.[[343]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199746-343) Other days, known as *ke-no-hi*, were generally avoided for festivities.[[343]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199746-343) However, since the late 20th century, many shrines have held their festival celebrations on the Saturday or Sunday closest to the date so that fewer individuals will be working and will be able to attend.[[344]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996224Earhart2004222-344) Each town or village often has its own festival, centred on a local shrine.[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200281-312) For instance, the [Aoi Matsuri](/wiki/Aoi_Matsuri "Aoi Matsuri") festival, held on 15 May to pray for an abundant grain harvest, takes place at shrines in [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto"),[[345]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking19976Picken201142-345) while the [Chichibu Night Festival](/wiki/Chichibu_Night_Festival "Chichibu Night Festival") takes place on 2–3 December in [Chichibu](/wiki/Chichibu,_Saitama "Chichibu, Saitama").[[346]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201159-346)

Spring festivals are called *haru-matsuri* and often incorporate prayers for a good harvest.[[343]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199746-343) They sometimes involve *ta-asobi* ceremonies, in which rice is ritually planted.[[343]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199746-343) Summer festivals are termed *natsu-matsuri* and are usually focused on protecting the crops against pests and other threats.[[347]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997132-347) Autumn festivals are known as *aki-matsuri* and primarily focus on thanking the *kami* for the rice or other harvest.[[348]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking19972Picken201135-348) The *[Niiname-sai](/wiki/Niiname-sai "Niiname-sai")*, or festival of new rice, is held across many Shinto shrines on 23 November.[[349]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996170-349) The emperor also conducts a ceremony to mark this festival, at which he presents the first fruits of the harvest to the *kami* at midnight.[[350]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979205-350) Winter festivals, called *fuyu no matsuri* often feature on welcoming in the spring, expelling evil, and calling in good influences for the future.[[351]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199732-351) There is little difference between winter festivals and specific new year festivals.[[351]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199732-351)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Tomioka_hachimangu10.jpg/250px-Tomioka_hachimangu10.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tomioka_hachimangu10.jpg)

Procession of the *kami* as part of the [Fukagawa Matsuri](/wiki/Fukagawa_Matsuri "Fukagawa Matsuri") festival in Tokyo

The [season of the new year](/wiki/Japanese_New_Year "Japanese New Year") is called *shogatsu*.[[352]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997182Littleton200280-352) On the last day of the year (31 December), *omisoka*, practitioners usually clean their household shrines in preparation for New Year's Day (1 January), *ganjitsu*.[[353]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997139-353) Many people visit public shrines to celebrate new year;[[354]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979205Nelson1996199Littleton200280BreenTeeuwen20103-354) this "first visit" of the year is known as *hatsumōde* or *hatsumairi*.[[355]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199747BreenTeeuwen20103-355) There, they buy amulets and talismans to bring them good fortune over the coming year.[[356]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996208-356) To celebrate this festival, many Japanese put up rope known as *[shimenawa](/wiki/Shimenawa "Shimenawa")* on their homes and places of business.[[357]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996206Bocking1997163-357) Some also put up *[kadomatsu](/wiki/Kadomatsu "Kadomatsu")* ("gateway pine"), an arrangement of pine branches, plum tree, and bamboo sticks.[[358]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996206Bocking199781-358) Also displayed are *kazari*, which are smaller and more colourful; their purpose is to keep away misfortune and attract good fortune.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199793-144) In many places, new year celebrations incorporate *[hadaka matsuri](/wiki/Hadaka_matsuri "Hadaka matsuri")* ("naked festivals") in which men dressed only in a *[fundoshi](/wiki/Fundoshi "Fundoshi")* loincloth engage in a particular activity, such as fighting over a specific object or immersing themselves in a river.[[359]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199741-359)

A common feature of festivals are processions or parades known as *gyōretsu*.[[360]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199739–40-360) These can be raucous, with many participants being drunk;[[361]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979205Nelson1996133-361) Breen and Teeuwen characterised them as having a "carnivalesque atmosphere".[[362]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen20104-362) They are often understood as having a regenerative effect on both the participants and the community.[[363]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996134-363) During these processions, the *kami* travel in portable shrines known as *[mikoshi](/wiki/Mikoshi "Mikoshi")*.[[364]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996140Bocking1997122Littleton200282BreenTeeuwen20104-364) In various cases the *mikoshi* undergo *hamaori* ("going down to the beach"), a process by which they are carried to the sea shore and sometimes into the sea, either by bearers or a boat.[[365]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199743-365) For instance, in the Okunchi festival held in the southwestern city of [Nagasaki](/wiki/Nagasaki "Nagasaki"), the *kami* of the [Suwa Shrine](/wiki/Suwa_Shrine_(Nagasaki) "Suwa Shrine (Nagasaki)") are paraded down to Ohato, where they are placed in a shrine there for several days before being paraded back to Suwa.[[366]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996152–154-366) These sorts of celebrations are often organized largely by members of the local community rather than by the priests themselves.[[362]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen20104-362)

### Rites of passage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Rites of passage")]

The formal recognition of events is given great importance in Japanese culture.[[367]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson199634-367) A common ritual, the *hatsumiyamairi*, entails a child's first visit to a Shinto shrine.[[368]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996161Bocking199747BreenTeeuwen20103Picken201187–88-368) A tradition holds that, if a boy he should be brought to the shrine on the thirty-second day after birth, and if a girl she should be brought on the thirty-third day.[[369]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199747-369) Historically, the child was commonly brought to the shrine not by the mother, who was considered impure after birth, but by another female relative; since the late 20th century it has been more common for the mother to do so.[[369]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199747-369) Another rite of passage, the *saiten-sai* or *seijin shiki*, is a coming of age ritual marking the transition to adulthood and occurs when an individual is around twenty.[[370]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996212–213Bocking1997156-370) Wedding ceremonies are often carried out at Shinto shrines;[[371]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart200415-371) these are called *shinzen kekkon* ("a wedding before the *kami*").[[372]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997178Picken201187-372) Prior to the Meiji era, weddings were commonly performed in the home,[[373]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997178–179-373) although shrines now regard them as an important source of income.[[374]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201187-374)

In Japan, funerals tend to take place at Buddhist temples and involve cremation,[[375]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200292Earhart200415-375) with Shinto funerals being rare.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201139-139) Bocking noted that most Japanese people are "still 'born Shinto' yet 'die Buddhist'."[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997ix-176) In Shinto thought, contact with death is seen as imparting impurity (*kegare*); the period following this contact is known as *kibuku* and is associated with various taboos.[[376]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199795-376) In cases when dead humans are enshrined as *kami*, the physical remains of the dead are not stored at the shrine.[[377]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201119-377) Although not common, there have been examples of funerals conducted through Shinto rites. The earliest examples are known from the mid-17th century; these occurred in certain areas of Japan and had the support of the local authorities.[[378]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenney2000241-378)
Following the Meiji Restoration, in 1868 the government recognised specifically Shinto funerals for Shinto priests.[[379]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997187Kenney2000240-379) Five years later, this was extended to cover the entire Japanese population.[[380]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenney2000240–241-380) Despite this Meiji promotion of Shinto funerals, the majority of the population continued to have Buddhist funeral rites.[[378]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenney2000241-378) In recent decades, Shinto funerals have usually been reserved for Shinto priests and for members of certain Shinto sects.[[381]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997188-381) After [cremation](/wiki/Cremation "Cremation"), the normal funerary process in Japan, the ashes of a priest may be interred near to the shrine, but not inside its precincts.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201171-128)

Ancestral reverence remains an important part of Japanese religious custom.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201139-139) The invocation of the dead, and especially the war dead, is known as *shōkon*.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997182-166) Various rites reference this. For instance, at the largely Buddhist festival of [Bon](/wiki/Bon_Festival "Bon Festival"), the souls of the ancestors are believed to visit the living, and are then sent away in a ritual called *shōrō nagashi*, by which lanterns are inserted into small boats, often made of paper, and placed in a river to float downstream.[[382]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997183-382)

### Spirit mediumship and healing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Spirit mediumship and healing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Inako_2006-10-09.jpg/250px-Inako_2006-10-09.jpg)](/wiki/File:Inako_2006-10-09.jpg)

An *itako* at the autumn Inako Taisai festival at [Mount Osore](/wiki/Mount_Osore "Mount Osore"), Aomori Prefecture, Japan

Shinto practitioners believe that the *kami* can possess a human being and then speak through them, a process known as *kami-gakari*.[[383]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199785–86-383) Several new religious movements drawing upon Shinto, such as [Tenrikyo](/wiki/Tenrikyo "Tenrikyo") and [Oomoto](/wiki/Oomoto "Oomoto"), were founded by individuals claiming to be guided by a possessing *kami*.[[384]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199786-384) The *takusen* is an [oracle](/wiki/Oracle "Oracle") that is passed from the *kami* via the medium.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997197-214)

The *[itako](/wiki/Itako "Itako")* and *ichiko* are blind women who train to become [spiritual mediums](/wiki/Mediumship "Mediumship"), traditionally in Japan's northern [Tohoku](/wiki/Tohoku "Tohoku") region.[[385]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199763-385) *Itako* train under other *itako* from childhood, memorialising sacred texts and prayers, fasting, and undertaking acts of severe asceticism, through which they are believed to cultivate supernatural powers.[[385]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199763-385) In an initiation ceremony, a *kami* is believed to possess the young woman, and the two are then ritually "married". After this, the *kami* becomes her tutelary spirit and she will henceforth be able to call upon it, and a range of other spirits, in the future. Through contacting these spirits, she is able to convey their messages to the living.[[385]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199763-385) *Itako* usually carry out their rituals independent of the shrine system.[[386]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199763–64-386) Japanese culture also includes spiritual healers known as *ogamiya-san* whose work involves invoking both *kami* and Buddhas.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997136-161)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Shinto](/wiki/History_of_Shinto "History of Shinto")

### Early development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Early development")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/DotakuBronzeBellLateYayoi3rdCenturyCE.jpg/250px-DotakuBronzeBellLateYayoi3rdCenturyCE.jpg)](/wiki/File:DotakuBronzeBellLateYayoi3rdCenturyCE.jpg)

A Yayoi period *dotaku* bell; these probably played a key role in *kami* rites at the time.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201719-81)

Earhart commented that Shinto ultimately "emerged from the beliefs and practices of prehistoric Japan",[[387]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart20042-387) although Kitagawa noted that it was questionable whether prehistoric Japanese religions could be accurately termed "early Shinto".[[322]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa198739-322) It was the [Yayoi period](/wiki/Yayoi_period "Yayoi period") of Japanese prehistory which first left traces of material and iconography prefiguring that later included in Shinto.[[388]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200214Hardacre201718-388) *Kami* were worshipped at various landscape features during this period; at this point, their worship consisted largely of beseeching and placating them, with little evidence that they were viewed as compassionate entities.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201719-81) [Archaeological](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology") evidence suggests that *[dotaku](/wiki/Dotaku "Dotaku")* bronze bells, bronze weapons, and metal mirrors played an important role in *kami*-based ritual during the [Yayoi period](/wiki/Yayoi_period "Yayoi period").[[389]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200215Hardacre201719-389)

In this early period, Japan was not a unified state; by the [Kofun period](/wiki/Kofun_period "Kofun period") it was divided among *[Uji](/wiki/Uji_(clan) "Uji (clan)")* (clans), each with their own tutelary *kami*, the *ujigami*.[[390]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200215Hardacre201724-390) Korean migration during the Kofun period brought Confucianism and Buddhism to Japan.[[391]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201723-391) Buddhism had a particular impact on the *kami* cults.[[392]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201724-392) Migrant groups and Japanese who increasingly aligned with these foreign influences built Buddhist temples in various parts of the Japanese islands.[[392]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201724-392) Several rival clans who were more hostile to these foreign influences began adapting the shrines of their *kami* to more closely resemble the new Buddhist structures.[[392]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201724-392) In the late 5th century, the [imperial dynasty](/wiki/Imperial_House_of_Japan "Imperial House of Japan") leader [Yūryaku](/wiki/Emperor_Y%C5%ABryaku "Emperor Yūryaku") declared himself *[daiō](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E3%81%A0%E3%81%84%E3%81%8A%E3%81%86 "wikt:だいおう")* ("great king") and established hegemony over much of Japan.[[393]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201725-393) From the early 6th century CE, the style of ritual favored by the [Yamato](/wiki/Yamato_people "Yamato people") began spreading to other *kami* shrines around Japan as the Yamato extended their territorial influence.[[394]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201727-394) Buddhism was also growing. According to the *Nihon Shoki*, in 587 [Emperor Yōmei](/wiki/Emperor_Y%C5%8Dmei "Emperor Yōmei") converted to Buddhism and under his sponsorship Buddhism spread.[[395]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201728-395)

In the mid-7th century, a legal code called *[Ritsuryō](/wiki/Ritsury%C5%8D "Ritsuryō")* was adopted to establish a Chinese-style centralised government.[[396]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201717-396) As part of this, the [Jingikan](/wiki/Jingikan "Jingikan") ("Council of *Kami*") was created to conduct rites of state and coordinate provincial ritual with that in the capital.[[397]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201717–18-397) This was done according to a code of *kami* law called the *Jingiryō*,[[397]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201717–18-397) itself modelled on the Chinese *[Book of Rites](/wiki/Book_of_Rites "Book of Rites")*.[[398]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201731-398) The Jingikan was located in the palace precincts and maintained a register of shrines and priests.[[399]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201733-399) An annual calendar of state rites were introduced to help unify Japan through *kami* worship.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201718-9) These legally mandated rites were outlined in the [Yōrō Code](/wiki/Y%C5%8Dr%C5%8D_Code "Yōrō Code") of 718,[[398]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201731-398) and expanded in the *Jogan Gishiki* of circa 872 and the *[Engi Shiki](/wiki/Engi_Shiki "Engi Shiki")* of 927.[[398]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201731-398) Under the Jingikan, some shrines were designated as *kansha* ("official shrines") and given specific privileges and responsibilities.[[400]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201733–34-400) Hardacre saw the Jingikan as "the institutional origin of Shinto".[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201718-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Shinpukuji-bon_Kojiki_%28%E7%9C%9F%E7%A6%8F%E5%AF%BA%E6%9C%AC%E5%8F%A4%E4%BA%8B%E8%A8%98%29.png/250px-Shinpukuji-bon_Kojiki_%28%E7%9C%9F%E7%A6%8F%E5%AF%BA%E6%9C%AC%E5%8F%A4%E4%BA%8B%E8%A8%98%29.png)](/wiki/File:Shinpukuji-bon_Kojiki_(%E7%9C%9F%E7%A6%8F%E5%AF%BA%E6%9C%AC%E5%8F%A4%E4%BA%8B%E8%A8%98).png)

A page from the 14th-century Shinpukuji manuscript of the *Kojiki*, itself written in the 8th century

In the early 8th century, the Emperor [Tenmu](/wiki/Tenmu "Tenmu") commissioned a compilation of the legends and genealogies of Japan's clans, resulting in the completion of the *Kojiki* in 712. Designed to legitimate the ruling dynasty, this text created a fixed version of various stories previously circulating in oral tradition.[[401]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201747–48-401) The *Kojiki* omits any reference to Buddhism,[[402]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201764-402) in part because it sought to ignore foreign influences and emphasise a narrative stressing indigenous elements of Japanese culture.[[403]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201768-403) Several years later, the *Nihon shoki* was written. Unlike the *Kojiki*, this made various references to Buddhism,[[402]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201764-402) and was aimed at a foreign audience.[[404]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201769-404) Both of these texts sought to establish the imperial clan's descent from the sun *kami* Amaterasu,[[402]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201764-402) although there were many differences in the cosmogonic narrative they provided.[[405]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201757–59-405) Quickly, the *Nihon shoki* eclipsed the *Kojiki* in terms of its influence.[[404]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201769-404) Other texts written at this time also drew on oral traditions regarding the *kami*. The *[Sendari kuji hongi](/wiki/Kujiki "Kujiki")* for example was probably composed by the [Mononobe](/wiki/Mononobe "Mononobe") clan while the *[Kogoshui](/wiki/Kogoshui "Kogoshui")* was probably put together for the [Imbe clan](/wiki/Imbe_clan "Imbe clan"), and in both cases they were designed to highlight the divine origins of these respective lineages.[[406]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201764–45-406) A government order in 713 called on each region to produce *[fudoki](/wiki/Fudoki "Fudoki")*, records of local geography, products, and stories, with the latter revealing more traditions about the *kami* which were present at this time.[[407]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton200243Hardacre201766-407)

From the 8th century, *kami* worship and Buddhism were thoroughly intertwined in Japanese society.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaliDougill20138-189) While the emperor and court performed Buddhist rites, they also performed others to honor the *kami*.[[408]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201772-408) Tenmu for example appointed a virginal imperial princess to serve as the *[Saiō](/wiki/Sai%C5%8D "Saiō")*, a form of priestess, at the Ise Shrine on his behalf, a tradition continued by subsequent emperors.[[409]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre201782–83-409) From the 8th century onward up until the [Meiji era](/wiki/Meiji_era "Meiji era"), the *kami* were incorporated into a Buddhist cosmology in various ways.[[410]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuroda19819-410) One view is that the *kami* realised that like all other life-forms, they too were trapped in the cycle of [samsara](/wiki/Samsara "Samsara") (rebirth) and that to escape this they had to follow Buddhist teachings.[[410]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuroda19819-410) Alternative approaches viewed the *kami* as benevolent entities who protected Buddhism, or that the *kami* were themselves [Buddhas](/wiki/Buddhas "Buddhas"), or beings who had achieved enlightenment. In this, they could be either *[hongaku](/wiki/Hongaku "Hongaku")*, the pure spirits of the Buddhas, or *[honji suijaku](/wiki/Honji_suijaku "Honji suijaku")*, transformations of the Buddhas in their attempt to help all sentient beings.[[410]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuroda19819-410)

### Nara period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Nara period")]

This period hosted many changes to the country, government, and religion. The capital is moved again to [Heijō-kyō](/wiki/Heij%C5%8D-ky%C5%8D "Heijō-kyō") (modern-day [Nara](/wiki/Nara,_Nara "Nara, Nara")), in AD 710 by [Empress Genmei](/wiki/Empress_Genmei "Empress Genmei") due to the death of the emperor. This practice was necessary due to the Shinto belief in the impurity of death and the need to avoid this pollution. However, this practice of moving the capital due to "death impurity" is then abolished by the [Taihō Code](/wiki/Taih%C5%8D_Code "Taihō Code") and rise in Buddhist influence.[[411]](#cite_note-JapaneseReligion1985-411) The establishment of the imperial city in partnership with Taihō Code is important to Shinto as the office of the Shinto rites becomes more powerful in assimilating local clan shrines into the imperial fold. New shrines are built and assimilated each time the city is moved. All of the grand shrines are regulated under [Taihō](/wiki/Taih%C5%8D_Code "Taihō Code") and are required to account for incomes, priests, and practices due to their national contributions.[[411]](#cite_note-JapaneseReligion1985-411)

### Meiji era and the Empire of Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Meiji era and the Empire of Japan")]

Main article: [State Shinto](/wiki/State_Shinto "State Shinto")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Chosen_Jingu.JPG/250px-Chosen_Jingu.JPG)](/wiki/File:Chosen_Jingu.JPG)

The [Chōsen Jingū](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Dsen_Jing%C5%AB "Chōsen Jingū") in [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul"), Korea, established during the Japanese occupation of the peninsula

Breen and Teeuwen characterise the period between 1868 and 1915, during the Meiji era, as being the "formative years" of modern Shinto.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen20107-10) It is in this period that various scholars have argued that Shinto was essentially "invented".[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen20107-10)
Fridell argues that scholars call the period from 1868 to 1945 the "State Shinto period" because, "during these decades, Shinto elements came under a great deal of overt state influence and control as the Japanese government systematically utilized shrine worship as a major force for mobilizing imperial loyalties on behalf of modern nation-building."[[412]](#cite_note-412) However, the government had already been treating shrines as an extension of government before Meiji; see for example the [Tenpō Reforms](/wiki/Tenp%C5%8D_Reforms "Tenpō Reforms"). Moreover, according to the scholar [Jason Ānanda Josephson](/wiki/Jason_Josephson_Storm "Jason Josephson Storm"), It is inaccurate to describe shrines as constituting a "state religion" or a "theocracy" during this period since they had neither organization, nor doctrine, and were uninterested in conversion.[[413]](#cite_note-413)

The [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration") of 1868 was fuelled by a renewal of Confucian ethics and imperial patriotism among Japan's ruling class.[[414]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen20108-414) Among these reformers, Buddhism was seen as a corrupting influence that had undermined what they envisioned as Japan's original purity and greatness.[[414]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen20108-414) They wanted to place a renewed emphasis on *kami* worship as an indigenous form of ritual, an attitude that was also fuelled by anxieties about Western expansionism and fear that Christianity would take hold in Japan.[[414]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen20108-414)

In 1868, all shrine priests were placed under the authority of the new [Jingikan](/wiki/Jingikan "Jingikan"), or Council of Kami Affairs.[[415]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen20107–8-415) A project of forcibly separating *kami* worship from Buddhism was implemented, with Buddhist monks, deities, buildings, and rituals banned from *kami* shrines.[[414]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen20108-414) Much Buddhist material was destroyed.[[414]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen20108-414) In 1871, a new hierarchy of shrines was introduced, with imperial and national shrines at the top.[[416]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen20109Azegami201271-416) Hereditary priesthoods were abolished and a new state-sanctioned system for appointing priests was introduced.[[417]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen20109-417)
In 1872, the Jingikan was replaced with the [Kyobusho](/w/index.php?title=Kyobusho&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kyobusho (page does not exist)"), or Ministry of Edification.[[418]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen201010-418) This coordinated [a campaign](/wiki/Great_Promulgation_Campaign "Great Promulgation Campaign") whereby *kyodoshoku* ("national evangelists") were sent through the country to promote Japan's "Great Teaching", which included respect for the *kami* and obedience to the emperor.[[418]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen201010-418) This campaign was discontinued in 1884.[[418]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen201010-418) In 1906, thousands of village shrines were merged so that most small communities had only a single shrine, where rites in honor of the emperor could be held.[[419]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen201011-419) Shinto effectively became the state cult, one promoted with growing zeal in the build-up to the Second World War.[[419]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen201011-419)

In 1882, the Meiji government designated 13 religious movements that were neither Buddhist nor Christian to be forms of "[Sect Shinto](/wiki/Sect_Shinto "Sect Shinto")".[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOffner1979215-38) The number and name of the sects given this formal designation varied;[[420]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997112-420) often they merged ideas with Shinto from Buddhism, Christian, Confucian, Daoist, and [Western esoteric](/wiki/Western_esotericism "Western esotericism") traditions.[[421]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELittleton2002100–101-421) In the Meiji period, many local traditions died out and were replaced by nationally standardised practices encouraged from Tokyo.[[422]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen201012-422)

### Post-war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Post-war")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Association_of_Shinto_Shrines_2010.jpg/250px-Association_of_Shinto_Shrines_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Association_of_Shinto_Shrines_2010.jpg)

The headquarters of the [Association of Shinto Shrines](/wiki/Association_of_Shinto_Shrines "Association of Shinto Shrines") in [Shibuya](/wiki/Shibuya "Shibuya"), [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo")

During the U.S. occupation, a [new Japanese constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Japan "Constitution of Japan") was drawn up. This enshrined [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion") and separated religion from the state, a measure designed to eradicate State Shinto.[[423]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUeda1979304Kitagawa1987171Bocking199718Earhart2004207-423) The emperor declared that he was not a *kami*;[[424]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart2004207-424) Shinto rituals performed by the imperial family became their own private affair.[[425]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUeda1979304-425) This disestablishment ended government subsidies to shrines and gave them renewed freedom to organise their own affairs.[[424]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart2004207-424) In 1946 many shrines formed a voluntary organisation, the [Association of Shinto Shrines](/wiki/Association_of_Shinto_Shrines "Association of Shinto Shrines") (*Jinja Honchō*).[[426]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199775Earhart2004207–208-426) In 1956 the association issued a creedal statement, the *keishin seikatsu no kōryō* ("general characteristics of a life lived in reverence of the *kami*"), to summarise what they regarded as Shinto's principles.[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199794-212) By the late 1990s around 80% of Japan's Shinto shrines were part of this association.[[427]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199776-427)

In the post-war decades, many Japanese blamed Shinto for encouraging the militarism which had led to defeat and occupation.[[424]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart2004207-424) Others remained nostalgic for State Shinto,[[428]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa1987172-428) and concerns were repeatedly expressed that sectors of Japanese society were conspiring to restore it.[[429]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201118-429) Various legal debates revolved around the involvement of public officials in Shinto.[[430]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking199718-430) In 1965, for instance, the city of [Tsu](/wiki/Tsu,_Mie "Tsu, Mie"), Mie Prefecture, paid four Shinto priests to purify the site where the municipal athletic hall was to be built. Critics brought the case to court, claiming it contravened the constitutional separation of religion and state; in 1971 the high court ruled that the city administration's act had been unconstitutional, although this was overturned by the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Japan "Supreme Court of Japan") in 1977.[[431]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUeda1979307Breen201071–72-431)

During the post-war period, Shinto themes often blended into Japanese [new religious movements](/wiki/New_religious_movement "New religious movement").[[432]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1996180-432) Of the Sect Shinto groups, [Tenrikyo](/wiki/Tenrikyo "Tenrikyo") was probably the most successful,[[428]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitagawa1987172-428) although in 1970 it repudiated its Shinto identity.[[433]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997113-433) Shinto perspectives also influenced popular culture. The film director [Hayao Miyazaki](/wiki/Hayao_Miyazaki "Hayao Miyazaki") of [Studio Ghibli](/wiki/Studio_Ghibli "Studio Ghibli") for instance acknowledged Shinto influences on his films such as *[Spirited Away](/wiki/Spirited_Away "Spirited Away")*.[[434]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoydNishimura20163-434) Shinto also spread abroad through both emigration and conversion by non-Japanese.[[435]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken2011xivSuga201048-435) The [Tsubaki Grand Shrine](/wiki/Tsubaki_Grand_Shrine "Tsubaki Grand Shrine") in [Suzuka](/wiki/Suzuka,_Mie "Suzuka, Mie"), Mie Prefecture, was the first to establish a branch abroad: the [Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America](/wiki/Tsubaki_Grand_Shrine_of_America "Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America"), initially located in California and then moved to [Granite Falls, Washington](/wiki/Granite_Falls,_Washington "Granite Falls, Washington").[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201132-243)

During the 20th century, most academic research on Shinto was conducted by Shinto theologians, often priests,[[436]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997176-436) bringing accusations that it often blurred theology with historical analysis.[[437]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre20174-437) From the 1980s onward, there was a renewed academic interest in Shinto both in Japan and abroad.[[438]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBocking1997177-438)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Demographics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Guji_Francesco_Brigante.jpg/250px-Guji_Francesco_Brigante.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guji_Francesco_Brigante.jpg)

A Shinto rite carried out at a jinja in [San Marino](/wiki/San_Marino "San Marino"), Europe

Most Japanese participate in several religious traditions,[[439]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart20044,_214-439) with Breen and Teeuwen noting that, "with few exceptions", it is not possible to differentiate between Shintoists and Buddhists in Japan.[[440]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen20102-440) The main exceptions are members of minority religious groups, including Christianity, which promote exclusive worldviews.[[441]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart2004215-441) Determining the proportions of the country's population who engage in Shinto activity is hindered by the fact that, if asked, Japanese people will often say "I have no religion".[[441]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarhart2004215-441) Many Japanese avoid the term "religion", in part because they dislike the connotations of the word which most closely matches it in the Japanese language, *shūkyō*. The latter term derives from *shū* ("sect") and *kyō* ("doctrine").[[442]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson19968-442)

Official statistics show Shinto to be Japan's largest religion, with over 80 percent of its population engaging in Shinto activities.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen20101-195)[[443]](#cite_note-:0-443) Conversely, in questionnaires only a small minority of Japanese describe themselves as "Shintoists".[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen20101-195) This indicates that a far larger number of people engage in Shinto activities than cite Shinto as their religious identity.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreenTeeuwen20101-195) There are no formal rituals to become a practitioner of "folk Shinto". Thus, "Shinto membership" is often estimated counting only those who do join organized Shinto sects.[[444]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamsBharMarty20044–5-444) Shinto has about 81,000 shrines and about 85,000 priests in the country.[[443]](#cite_note-:0-443) According to surveys carried out in 2006[[445]](#cite_note-Dentsu2006-445) and 2008,[[446]](#cite_note-NHK2008-446) less than 40% of the population of Japan identifies with an organised religion: around 35% are [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), 30% to 40% are members of [Shinto sects and derived religions](/wiki/Shinto_sects_and_schools#Shintō_inspired_religions "Shinto sects and schools"). In 2008, 26% of the participants reported often visiting Shinto shrines, while only 16.2% expressed belief in the existence of *kami* in general.[[446]](#cite_note-NHK2008-446)

### Shinto outside Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Shinto outside Japan")]

Main article: [Overseas Shinto](/wiki/Overseas_Shinto "Overseas Shinto")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Karafuto_Shrine.JPG/250px-Karafuto_Shrine.JPG)](/wiki/File:Karafuto_Shrine.JPG)

The Karafuto Shrine in the Sakhalin Region

The end of the 19th and beginning of the 20th centuries were marked by the expansion of the [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan"), which also led to the spread of Shinto in the colonized territories.[[447]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre2017431–432-447) In total, from 1868 to 1945, 1,640 shrines were built in territories under Japanese control.[[447]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre2017431–432-447)[[448]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENakajima201022-448) In addition, starting in 1885, Japanese began to move to [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii"), most of whom left Japan for economic reasons; Since 1908, [emigration to Brazil](/wiki/Japanese_Brazilians "Japanese Brazilians") also began, where the Japanese worked on coffee plantations. The emigrants built shrines to preserve their culture and worship traditional deities.[[449]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken2011273–274-449)[[450]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardacre2017404–406-450)

*Jinja* outside Japan are termed *kaigai jinja* ("overseas shrines"), a term coined by [Ogasawara Shozo](/w/index.php?title=Ogasawara_Shozo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ogasawara Shozo (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%B0%8F%E7%AC%A0%E5%8E%9F%E7%9C%81%E4%B8%89 "ja:小笠原省三")].[[451]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESuga201048-451) When the Empire of Japan collapsed in the 1940s, there were over 600 *jinja* within its conquered territories, many of which were later disbanded.[[451]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESuga201048-451) Japanese migrants have also established *jinja* in countries like Brazil,[[452]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESuga201059–60-452) while Shinto's lack of doctrinal focus has attracted interest from non-Japanese;[[453]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken2011xiv-453) in the United States, for example, [European Americans](/wiki/European_Americans "European Americans") have played a significant role in introducing Shinto.[[453]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken2011xiv-453)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinto&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

Shinto features in popular culture as folk Shinto or *Minkan Shinto*.[[454]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicken201189,_194-454)
